posts.caption:
WEST’S SILENT OUTCRY
“It’s basically like the inside of my brain. It tells the story from when I was about 19 to now. It explores five different emotions, each just as intense as the others.
It’s called Man’s Ruin.” — Westislonely
Following the release of her new single ‘Bad Dog’ and ahead of a new EP due this July, Westislonely presents music rooted in the spirit of punk. Through striking black-and-white imagery, her music embodies the energy of rock, expressing her inner emotions with raw honesty.
The Westislonely full editorial will be available on our website soon.

posts.caption:
Every summer, the @serpentineuk commissions a world-renowned architect to create the Serpentine Pavilion, a temporary architectural installation in Kensington Gardens, London. This year, the 25th pavilion is ‘a serpentine’ by Mexican architecture studio @lanzaatelier, founded by Isabel Abascal and Alessandro Arienzo.
Inspired by the traditional curved brick structure known as crinkle-crankle walls, commonly found in Suffolk, England, ‘a serpentine’ reinterprets a familiar local element in a contemporary way. The winding wall that stretches across one side of the pavilion naturally evokes the outline of the nearby Serpentine Lake. Once you step beneath the brick columns and the translucent roof, it feels as though you’ve entered a quiet woodland retreat. On rainy summer days, it offers shelter from the weather, while on sunny days, it provides a cool, shaded place to pause and rest. The chairs and stools placed throughout the pavilion were also designed by Lanza Atelier using locally sourced hardwood. Their approach—treating architecture and furniture as part of a single experience—adds another layer of warmth and comfort to the space.
Through ‘a serpentine’, Lanza Atelier transforms the familiar material of brick through local history, landscape, and their own architectural approach. It feels like the kind of place that could become a special retreat for anyone spending the summer in London.
📌Serpentine Pavilion
• Date: 6 June - 25 Oct
• Location: Serpentine South Gallery, W2 3XA
Words by Julie Jeong
Edited by Josephine Jiang
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.

posts.caption:
Even if you’ve never worn @47 Clean Up Cap before, you rarely wear it just once. A signature piece of ‘47, known as the #DadHat, the #CleanUpCap features a curved visor and classic strap-back design, delivering both comfort and timeless style. It moves easily between occasions — from sporty and casual looks to more refined styling.
It’s not really about style codes or dress codes — it just works wherever it goes. This campaign was shot across five locations — France, Italy, Mexico, Scotland, and Texas — highlighting how the Clean Up Cap naturally belongs in a global context. Swipe right to see it across five different locations.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.

posts.caption:
Who is Talia Chetrit?
You might not have heard her name, but you have certainly seen her work. The American photographer is behind LOEWE’s recent 180th anniversary campaign, as well as the sensual imagery for Jack McCollough and Lazaro Hernandez’s first campaign as creative directors. She has also created striking images for Phoebe Philo, Celine, Saint Laurent and Acne Studios.
Beyond fashion, Chetrit has built a deeply personal body of work around self-portraits, family, sexuality, relationships and female self-representation.
Her images often feel intimate but unsettling, familiar but slightly off. Whether working in fashion or fine art, Chetrit uses photography to question what it means to look, to pose, and to become the subject of an image.
Swipe to discover a beautiful collection of Talia Chetrit’s work.
Words by Josephine Jiang
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @taliachetrit, @loewe, @phoebephilo, @ysl

posts.caption:
@hiromimusic’s relationship with the piano has always been intuitive. Born in Hamamatsu, Shizuoka, in 1979, the Japanese pianist and composer was already performing with the Czech Philharmonic by the age of 14. Three years later, after meeting Chick Corea by chance in Tokyo, she was invited to join him on stage the following night.
Since then, Hiromi has built one of contemporary jazz’s most animated careers, moving between solo piano, electric fusion and small-ensemble work with a style that is both technically formidable and emotionally unguarded.
In May 2026, Hiromi performed a number of her songs at @miumiu Jazz Club in Tokyo held at Dance Hall Shinseiki. Following her performance, the virtuoso pianist and composer spoke with @georgepistachio about  the unorthodox teachings of her childhood tutor Noriko Hikida.
Read more at the link in bio 📲
📸 Portrait: Hiromi at Miu Miu Jazz Club. Photography by @sshotakonoo

posts.caption:
Contracted to Vogue from 1981 to 1989, American photographer @sheilametzner’s poetic visual language, achieved in part by the laborious, almost alchemical, Fresson printing method, is synonymous with fashion photography from that decade. Born in 1939, the photographer’s portraits of people such as Uma Thurman, Robert Mapplethorpe and Willem Dafoe, alongside commissions for brands including Ralph Lauren, Fendi and Shiseido have been the subject of several monographs and exhibitions.⁠
⁠
C’est Chic, a new exhibition at @lagalerierougeparis in Paris pairs Metzner’s work with that of photographer, Lillian Bassman (1917-2012). Although working decades apart, this curatorial proposition suggests that the pair have much in common. At the link in bio, Adam Murray speaks with Mentzner about her photography 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Sheila Metzner and Lillian Bassman: C’est Chic. © Sheila Metzner. Courtesy of La Galerie Rouge⁠

posts.caption:
Photographer @seeing_itself is perhaps best known for documenting the nuances of Tokyo living. Capturing people, young and old, from the city’s centre to its suburban districts and the landscapes that surround it, Homma uses his camera to explore urban living, youth culture and the quotidian.⁠
⁠
A far cry from Homma’s city work is New Waves, an ongoing project in which the photographer, now 64 years old, refocuses his camera, shifting away from the populace to the sea and sky. Mostly photographed on the north shore of Oahu, Hawaii, each photograph depicts a wave as it approaches land, capturing the inconsistency of the ocean.⁠
⁠
Some of Homma’s wave images are on display in @theofficialselfridges as part of a temporary space hosted by @celine, also featuring pieces from Été Celine and Automne 2026. Homma’s work draws a neat parallel with Michael Rider’s vision for the brand, which rejects concept and tradition. “Rider simply does what he does,” Alexander Fury wrote in a review of the Celine Autumn/Winter 2026 show. ⁠
⁠
At the link in bio, @rose_dodd speaks to Homma about the photo series, the ocean and what he hopes people will discover from his images 📲⁠
⁠
📸 New Waves by #TakashiHomma. Photography by Takashi Homma. Courtesy of #Celine⁠

posts.caption:
“Wondering is underestimated,” @susannahfrankel tells @hube.magazine’s @alekskovaleva. ⁠
⁠
As editor-in-chief of AnOther Magazine for the past decade, Frankel has guided the publication into a new era. In conversation, she reflects on a career spanning Blitz, The Guardian and The Independent, before arriving at AnOther in 2001. Discussing the designers, photographers and collaborators who shaped her perspective – from Lee Alexander McQueen and Hussein Chalayan to Rei Kawakubo and Karl Lagerfeld – Frankel also considers the realities of independent publishing and the changing role of criticism today.⁠
⁠
“AnOther is not only a fashion magazine. It is built around culture more broadly,” Frankel tells hube. “But fashion, in a cultural context, is at its core. For me, it has always been about the designers.”⁠
⁠
Hube’s interview with this magazine’s editor-in-chief at the link in bio 📲

posts.caption:
Winking to 2016’s formative fashion moments and a childhood as a competitive freestyle disco dancer, @mafcsm student @oliverjeanes’s second collection finds beauty in bad taste. ⁠
⁠
“[Freestyle disco dancing] is mental,” he tells @viiolethayward. “Eight girls and I would go on the floor in the most insane, over-the-top rhinestone costumes you’ve ever seen in your life and a questionable amount of fake tan. You’d go, throw yourself around the floor, and hope that one of the judges standing around would notice you.” ⁠
⁠
Designed ahead of starting his MA at Central Saint Martins, Jeanes’ second collection builds on his penchant for ironesty, and this time, his experience as a dancer is in the spotlight. Read more at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
Photography: @zacharielewertoff. Creative consultant: @darrianagrande. Casting: @laurenvanmeeuwen. Hair: @mthr0905. Make-up: @_mahomoriyama_. Set design: @2cult. Lighting assistant: @caeldevers. Photography assistant: @samuelcgurung. Styling assistant: @minnadebagota

posts.caption:
Atelier Versace is officially returning to the Paris Haute Couture schedule in early 2027. The comeback will be led by newly appointed Creative Director Pieter Mulier, marking a new era for the house.
In honor of the announcement, revisiting Gianni Versace’s Spring 1995 couture collection - one of the clearest expressions of Atelier Versace at its peak.
Shown at the Hôtel Ritz in Paris, the collection moved between crystal-covered gowns, sculpted tailoring, and liquid silhouettes with the kind of glamour only Gianni could create. More than 30 years later, it still feels impossibly modern. Meanwhile, we are patiently awaiting the spirit of Gianni’s return.👑

posts.caption:
Donatella Versace and iconic Vogue editors Anna Wintour and Franca Sozzani: Circa 2000 versus together at Versace fashion show Autumn / Winter 2009, Milan Fashion Week.
These three women ruled the global fashion landscape at the turn of the millennium. As top editors, Wintour and Sozzani held massive influence over consumer taste and media coverage, making their consistent front-row and backstage presence at Donatella’s Milan shows vital to cementing Versace’s global status.

posts.caption:
In an industry where surnames are inherited currencies, Bazaar India’s April-May cover star, Tabu (@tabutiful), has operated for over three decades on a mononym. To understand her career is to understand what it meant to say yes to things the industry was not ready for and to have the instinct to recognise those things as worthy, even when no template existed for them. But she refuses to call it rebellion. “It was simply a reflection of who I am. The roles I chose, the decisions I made—they came from some similar place throughout my life. Some part of me has been completely unchanged,” she insists.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)
Interview: Malika Halder (@malikahalder)
Photographer: Soujit Das (@soujit.das)
Stylist: Gopalika Virmani (@gopalikavirmani)

posts.caption:
Marlo Kelly is on the cusp of bona fide international stardom. The angel-faced Australian actor stars in the Emmy Award-nominated sci-fi series #3BodyProblem, and speaks to BAZAAR from Budapest, where she is preparing to start shooting season three. From a school production of ‘Macbeth’ and ‘Home and Away’ to her current role as “the scary lady from the show”, Kelly recounts the humble – and serendipitous – start of her acting career. “It feels like an idea that I was born with,” shares the self-described “theatre nerd”.
Through the link in bio, see @marlokelly photographed back home in Australia and continue reading the digital cover story.

posts.caption:
“It is the luckiest thing that’s ever happened in my life,” says @marlokelly of the moment a talent agent signed her following her performance as Lady Macbeth in a school production. Upending the usual trope, the actor’s parents were supportive of her prioritising art over academics, on occasion. “One time [in year nine], I was worried about exams as well as a play,” she recalls. “And [my dad] was like, ‘don’t worry about your exams – just learn your lines.”
At the link in bio, continue reading BAZAAR’s interview with the #3BodyProblem star.

posts.caption:
There was something in the air in 2016.
At the height of the direct-to-consumer boom, so-called disruptor brands like Everlane, Glossier, Allbirds and Outdoor Voices were at the top of their game. This cohort of high-profile startups was billed as retail’s future.
More quietly, a different group of fashion startups was emerging. For the most part, these primarily women-led apparel businesses eschewed the playbook of the era. Ten years in, while the DTC stalwarts have all but faded from their former glory, many of these businesses are thriving.
BoF’s Diana Pearl (@dianaspearl) explores how rejecting the high-growth DTC playbook has kept brands like Dôen and La Ligne thriving. Read more #linkinbio
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by The Business of Fashion on June 03, 2026 tagging @mikedsykes. May be an image of one or more people, magazine, flight jacket, fleece and text that says 'BOF LI-MINYG LI-INIYG L1- CURRY ATиu TRa &Posselte Possible തലം THE KICKS YOU WEAR China's Li- Li-Ning Scores Big By Signing Stephen Curry BY MIKE SYKES'.

posts.caption:
Just when you thought the sneaker and sportswear market in the West couldn’t get any more crowded, here comes NBA superstar Stephen Curry with a deal that will insert a new power player into the marketplace.
After a months long free agency following his split from Under Armour where he courted the biggest sportswear brands in the world, including Nike and Adidas, Curry ultimately decided to bring his Curry Brand to Chinese sportswear company Li-Ning.
Curry’s 10-year deal with the brand includes an expansive assortment of products, and even a signature golf line, according to reporting from ESPN’s Shams Charania. He’ll also be able to sign other signature athletes under his label.
In BoF’s latest The Kicks You Wear newsletter, Mike Sykes (@mikedsykes) share his thoughts on Stephen Curry’s new home. Read more and subscribe #linkinbio
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Business of Fashion on June 03, 2026. May be an image of magazine and text that says 'и BOF For These Brands, Resisting the DTC Playbook Paid PlaybookPaidOff Off'.

posts.caption:
Brand management firms are bottom feeders no more.
Once considered to be a fashion graveyard, companies like Authentic Brands Group and Marquee Brands were known for acquiring troubled labels like Herve Leger, BCBG Maxazria and Forever 21 at bargain prices in order to juice every last dollar they could from them.
But these firms’ recent acquisitions are different — culturally relevant and even more or less financially healthy.
Read the full explainer on how brand management firms are moving upmarket #linkinbio
✍️ @austintaekim
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Business of Fashion on June 03, 2026. May be an image of magazine and text that says 'и BOF For These Brands, Resisting the DTC Playbook Paid PlaybookPaidOff Off'.

posts.caption:
Shoppers’ thirst for beauty is strong, but only the most innovative players are growing.
Beauty's recent earnings season showed a stark difference between companies who deploy trending innovations and deliver on value for money, and those who are still catching up. There's also a gulf between simply launching new products and connecting with customers — if new drops aren't targeted and considered, they'll underperform.
Read the full story on why innovation has become beauty's new north star. #linkinbio
✍️ @danielakmorosini

posts.caption:
Last week, Italian fashion lobby CNMI held the sixth edition of its grant programme for emerging designers, the Camera Moda Fashion Trust.
Galib Gassanoff’s Institution won for the second time. The designer came into the competition with taste, originality, momentum and a clear plan for using the funds.
Act No.1, a project formerly tied to Gassanoff which is being carried on by co-founder Luca Lin, was another repeat winner. Accessible fashion concept Materia also secured a grant for its collection of tech- and sports-wear, designed to be more ergonomic for persons with disabilities who want to dress themselves without assistance.
As a new generation of Italian fashion creatives presented their projects, Robert Williams noted a few common threads. Read his full thoughts in this week's High Margin newsletter #linkinbio
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Business of Fashion on June 03, 2026. May be an image of magazine and text that says 'и BOF For These Brands, Resisting the DTC Playbook Paid PlaybookPaidOff Off'.

posts.caption:
Neither Brooke DeVard nor Erica Chidi has a typical editor’s resume. Both can tout podcasting experience, but DeVard largely spent her career as a marketer for companies like Instagram and Ralph Lauren, while Chidi founded the reproductive health app Loom.
Refinery29 joins a growing list of women’s publications that are adopting a “if you can’t beat ‘em, join ‘em,” strategy in a bid to keep up with how young people consume media. Several legacy titles are working more closely with online creators or trying to turn their staffers into them.
BoF’s Diana Pearl (@dianaspearl) speaks to DeVard and Chidi about betting on creators, reinventing Refinery29 and the future of women’s media. Read more #linkinbio
📷 Courtesy @refinery29

posts.caption:
A trained ballet and contemporary dancer, our latest cover star Sanya Malhotra (@sanyamalhotra_) is an “unconventional” actor who pirouetted her way into hearts with numerous relatable roles—and many a viral reel—carving a unique niche for herself in the industry along the way. Brides Today gets a glimpse into her universe.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Our cover star opts for cinema that sparks conversations, takes pride in her individuality, and fiercely protects her mental peace. “Today, there’s clarity in my choices. I’m shedding old versions of myself to make space for newer, riskier, more interesting ones,” she says, unfiltered, unassuming and refreshingly candid. Bhumi Satish Pednekkar welcomes Brides Today (@bhumisatishpednekkar) into her world of stylish experiments, socially resonant movies, sustainable choices, and the legacy she hopes to leave behind.
Check out the complete cover story in the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Nothing says cocktail night glamour quite like a statement saree moment. And these celebs are serving all the inspiration we need this wedding season, from Ananya Panday’s (@ananyapanday) crystal-studded drape to Shraddha Kapoor’s (@shraddhakapoor) understated shimmer and Janhvi Kapoor’s (@janhvikapoor) statement pearl detailing. Think sculpted blouses, metallic accents, sequins, and fluid silhouettes made for glamorous sangeet and cocktail nights. Whether your style leans soft and elegant or bold and sparkly, these looks prove there’s a cocktail saree for every vibe. Consider this your cue to bring the drama to your next wedding function.
📷: Celebrity Handles
#BridesTodayIn #BridesTodayIndia

posts.caption:
Dua Lipa (@dualipa) just shared a glimpse into her intimate wedding celebrations with Callum Turner and every frame feels more beautiful than the last. Shot in black and white, the intimate photos capture the singer in a sleek ivory gown paired with a dramatic sculptural hat, serving modern bridal elegance with an old-Hollywood touch, while Callum’s classic tailoring and understated charm perfectly complement her. From the stolen kisses to the hand-in-hand moments, every photo feels effortlessly elegant and deeply romantic—and we’re completely obsessed.
📷: Dua Lipa
#BridesToday #BridesTodayIndia

posts.caption:
Few people do understated charm quite like Vedang Raina (@vedangraina). Whether he’s leaving an impression on screen in films like ‘The Archies’ and ‘Jigra’ or effortlessly carrying every Indian ethnic look with elegance, there’s a certain ease to his presence that always stands out. With his soft smile, classic style, and quietly charming energy, he continues to be a favourite for every mood board and screen alike. Wishing Vedang Raina the happiest birthday and a beautiful year ahead.
📷: @communiquefilmpr
#BridesToday #BridesTodayIndia

posts.caption:
Shikhar Dhawan’s (@shikhardofficial) birthday post for Sophie Shine Dhawan (@sophiedhawan93) was warm, heartfelt, and packed with the sweetest little moments. From cosy selfies to candid couple pictures, it gave us a glimpse into their loved-up world. And that heartfelt “most special blessing” note? We’re not getting over it anytime soon—exactly the kind of soft, happy love we’re all trying to manifest in our lives.
📷: Shikhar Dhawan
#BridesTodayIn #BridesTodayIndia

posts.caption:
The world's most famous blonde bombshell, perched on playground equipment, absorbed in a book. The studio make-up and lighting is gone; as are her shoes. It's 1955 and a summery glow radiates from her exposed limbs.
The woman, of course, is a 29-year-old Marilyn Monroe, captured by American photojournalist Eve Arnold in Long Island, New York. Through Arnold's lens, the Hollywood icon is quiet, contemplative and natural. Is Monroe aware of the camera? That's up for debate.
Arnold's photo tells a lesser-known story of Monroe; an avid reader who had a personal library of more than 400 books spanning poetry, plays, philosophy and dense literature like "Ulysses." And no, the book wasn't a prop, said Michael Arnold, grandson of the photographer who died in 2012. "Eve was just setting up her cameras, and she saw that Monroe got it out and was reading it, waiting for her to get ready," he said to CNN.
Look closer, and Monroe is on the last pages — where the protagonist's wife in the novel, Molly Bloom, explores female sexuality in an unpunctuated stream of consciousness. "With her choice to be seen reading the end of 'Ulysses,' Monroe was clearly making a knowing point," wrote leading feminist art historian Griselda Pollock in her 2016 essay on the photo, published in the Journal of Visual Culture. It was "an identification perhaps at so many levels with the words, the spoken words of an uneducated woman, allowed to have an inner and a sexual life, and to have the final say," added Pollock.
📸 : Eve Arnold Estate/Courtesy National Portrait Gallery
posts.alt_text: Photo by CNN Style on June 02, 2026. May be an image of ‎standing, kilt, costume, turtleneck, magazine and ‎text that says '‎ت 10 PARI PARIS VRARIS ١ A CLOSER LOOK AT PATRICK KELLY'S TRAGICALLY SHORT OUTSIZED INFLUENCE ON FASHION CNN‎'‎‎.

posts.caption:
Before the likes of Telfar Clemens, @laquan_smith, or the late Virgil Abloh became fashion industry forces, Patrick Kelly blazed a trail of his own.
In just the six short years when his formal ready-to-wear line was actively in production, Kelly landed a six-page spread in ELLE magazine, maintained a robust clientele that included everyone from @madonna to Cicely Tyson and even Princess Diana, and became the first Black designer to be inducted into what is now known as the Federation de la Haute Couture, France's governing fashion body.
"Love, Patrick: Nothing is Impossible," a documentary focusing on the designer, will premiere at several film festivals later this summer and offers an intimate look at Kelly's career, charting his unlikely success and tragic death at 35 due to complications from AIDS.
The movie features rarely seen clips from his high-energy fashion shows across Atlanta, New York, and Paris, that starred supermodels like @patcleveland @naomi Campbell and @the_real_iman, and interviews with those who worked alongside him.
"What really struck me was the overall plot of this project – that a Southern boy from Vicksburg, Mississippi, made it so big in the world of fashion. That's the type of underdog story that I love," said Ryon Horne, who along with his brothers Byron and Tyson directed "Love, Patrick."
"Something that (Kelly's partner) Bjorn Amelan says in the movie is that Patrick smiled a lot, but there was something behind that smile," Horne continued. "And in this film you will find out what that truly was. We want people to find out about the real Patrick Kelly … not just the bullet points."
📸 : John van Hasselt/Sygma/Corbis/PL Gould/Images Press/Getty Images
posts.alt_text: Photo by CNN Style on June 03, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, people smoking, poster, magazine and text that says 'MARILYN MONROE THROUGH THE FEMALE GAZE CNN'.

posts.caption:
This is @dualipa's week.
Not only because she is our Look of the Week, but because the next seven days – if a swirl of reports are to be believed – will be bookended by her nuptials.
On Sunday, just as north London was being flare-painted red at a parade for triumphant local soccer team @arsenal after winning the english premiership season, the pop star and her partner, British actor Callum Turner, headed to the quiet of west London, where Lipa's bridal whites would be kept pristine, for a secret civil ceremony at Old Marylebone Town Hall. The pair will reportedly next head to the Italian island of Sicily for a more flamboyant wedding party later this week.
The bar for bridal glamour was set high with her sophisticated, low-key register office look. Lipa descended the town hall steps hand-in-hand with Turner in a custom couture @schiaparelli skirt suit complete with "personalized gold bijoux buttons," per the brand. The singer's hips were padded, her hands were gloved and her face artfully shaded by a giant hat designed by @stephenjonesmillinery that was lined with golf leaf. Turner meanwhile was dressed in a navy double-breasted suit by @ferragamo.
Lipa, who is usually styled by @lorenzoposocco was never going to be your standard bride. She's a disciple of boom-boom fashion – animal print, fur, strong tailoring and glitz that flirts with garish. Her taste is a blend of '80s silhouettes and accessorizing principles refracted through the lens of modern designers such as @matthieu_blazy, @simon_porte_jacquemus and @chemena Kamali.
📸 : Associated Newspapers Ltd/Shutterstock
posts.alt_text: Photo by CNN Style on June 03, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, people smoking, poster, magazine and text that says 'MARILYN MONROE THROUGH THE FEMALE GAZE CNN'.

posts.caption:
When photographer Greg Brennan spotted Kate Moss in a fur coat at the bottom of a fire exit, cigarette in hand, he knew he'd stumbled across something special.
The year was 2007, and what he didn't realize is that one of the resulting photos — not even his favorite from the night — would become an emblem of the supermodel's "party era" and the best-known image of his near-four-decade career.
The mid-aughts photo's enduring appeal is, partly, its mundanity. In that quiet, unguarded moment, Moss was just like any other 30-something having a night out on the town.
And yet she is perhaps the only person who could appear that put-together while being ambushed in a stairwell. "It's kind of a mixture between a ballerina and Janis Joplin," Brennan said. "It's very rock 'n' roll."
Not all was as it seemed, though.
📸 : Greg Brennan
posts.alt_text: Photo by CNN Style on June 03, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, people smoking, poster, magazine and text that says 'MARILYN MONROE THROUGH THE FEMALE GAZE CNN'.

posts.caption:
When Icelandic Chinese musician Laufey dropped her latest music video last month, starring a cast of mixed white and Asian celebrities, the internet went wild.
"Laufey collecting wasians like pokemon," read one social media comment, using a portmanteau to describe people of mixed white and Asian descent. "WASIAN AVENGERS," another comment cheered.
She is among those who have publicly embraced the Wasian label — which has helped many people who self-identify as such to feel more seen.
This heightened attention coincides with a growing fascination in the West around Asian entertainment and culture (think the Netflix smash hit "KPop Demon Hunters," the recent Chinamaxxing trend, K-beauty products, and even Labubus). Increased visibility has crossed over into the real world, with "Wasian meetups" taking place earlier this May in San Francisco and New York City.
For some, it was a powerful and positive experience to see themselves represented and to connect with others.
But the meetups have also triggered a heated debate about race and identity that has reached a fever pitch online in recent weeks. The controversy has raised questions about who is represented, and why — as well as what it means to be multiracial in America.
Tap the link in @cnn’s bio for more.
📸 Nicole Mago/Warren Fu/Quentin Nguyen-duy
📹 Quentin Nguyen-duy

posts.caption:
By 2004, Britney Spears was no longer just pop’s biggest star, she had become one of the most talked-about women in the world. Her Blend magazine editorial captured that exact moment. Gone was the girl-next-door image that launched her career. In its place was a more confident, provocative and self-assured Britney, embracing a new chapter both personally and professionally.
The shoot perfectly reflected the mood of the mid-2000s: low-rise denim, leopard prints, smoky eyes and unapologetic glamour. But beyond the fashion, the editorial marked a shift in how Britney was perceived. She was no longer being presented as America’s sweetheart — she was creating her own image, on her own terms. The photos felt bolder, more mature and more rebellious than the carefully polished pop-star persona audiences had known before.
Looking back, the editorial feels like a snapshot of a turning point. It captured Britney at the height of her cultural influence, when every appearance, outfit and magazine cover became headline news. More than twenty years later, the images remain a reminder of a moment when Britney Spears wasn’t just following pop culture — she was defining it.
Editorial — Blend Magazine, 2004
Cover Star — Britney Spears
Photography — Rankin
Magazine — Blend Magazine
Year — 2004
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on June 02, 2026. May be an image of leather, magazine and text that says 'From the Archives: Balmain Spring 2011 CULT'.

posts.caption:
Looking back at Balmain Spring/Summer 2011, it’s easy to understand why the collection became so influential. Under Christophe Decarnin, Balmain was operating at full volume: sharp shoulders, tiny dresses, distressed denim, military details, crystal embellishments and enough attitude to make every look feel larger than life.
What made this era of Balmain so exciting was that it never tried to be understated. The collection embraced glamour, excess and confidence without apologizing for any of it. Every outfit felt designed for someone who wanted to be seen. The clothes weren’t necessarily realistic, but they were unforgettable.
More than a decade later, the collection feels surprisingly relevant again. Fashion has slowly moved away from overly polished minimalism and back toward statement dressing, strong silhouettes and a little bit of chaos. The embellished jackets, micro shorts and rockstar energy that defined Balmain in the early 2010s suddenly feel very aligned with fashion’s current obsession with glamour and personality.
Whether you love it or hate it, this was a period when fashion wasn’t afraid of being loud.
Collection — Balmain Spring/Summer 2011 Ready-to-Wear
Designer — Christophe Decarnin
Brand — Balmain
Presentation — Paris Fashion Week Spring/Summer 2011
Models — Including Abbey Lee Kershaw, Sasha Pivovarova, Anja Rubik, Isabeli Fontana and others
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on June 03, 2026. May be an image of magazine and text that says 'This Editorial Changed Britneys Image Forever CULT®'.

posts.caption:
Before Gigi Hadid became one of fashion’s biggest commercial stars, editorials like this showed why the industry was so interested in her in the first place.
Photographed by Steven Meisel, the story feels very clean and slightly weird in the way only Meisel can make fashion look. Rather than focusing on clothes alone, the editorial builds an atmosphere of joy and ease.
What makes the story work is the contrast between Gigi’s classic American beauty and Meisel’s more dramatic, narrative-driven approach to photography. Instead of looking like a model simply posing for the camera, she feels like a character moving through a larger story. That’s where the editorial succeeds: it transforms fashion into something people actually remember.
Looking back, it’s also a reminder of a period when major fashion magazines still invested heavily in storytelling, elaborate productions and strong visual identities. The result is an editorial that feels just as striking today as it did a decade ago.
Publication — Vogue Italia, November 2015
Photography — Steven Meisel
Model — Gigi Hadid
Styling — Karl Templer
Hair — Guido Palau
Makeup — Pat McGrath
Casting — Piergiorgio Del Moro
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on June 03, 2026. May be an image of magazine and text that says 'This Editorial Changed Britneys Image Forever CULT®'.

posts.caption:
Amy Winehouse embodied everything, Westwood had spent decades celebrating: individuality, rebellion, and the refusal to fit in.
Long before “punk” became a fashion trend, Vivienne Westwood built her career around outsiders. Amy was exactly that. With her towering beehive, winged eyeliner, tattoos, ballet flats, and unapologetically chaotic glamour, she created a style that felt entirely her own. It wasn’t calculated, and that’s precisely why it mattered.
Westwood admired people who challenged convention, and Amy did it effortlessly. She mixed vintage dresses with punk attitude, combined old Hollywood references with London’s underground music scene, and made imperfection look cooler than perfection ever could. In many ways, she represented the spirit of Westwood more authentically than many models who walked her runway.
Their connection became even more visible in 2011 when Amy appeared on the cover of Blitz, wearing Vivienne Westwood shortly before her death. After Amy passed away, Westwood publicly spoke about her admiration for the singer, describing her as beautiful, talented, and uniquely original.
What made Amy so special wasn’t that she followed fashion—it was that fashion followed her. Designers, stylists, and entire generations tried to recreate the effortless cool she possessed naturally. While many celebrities wore Vivienne Westwood, Amy Winehouse lived the values behind the clothes.
That’s why she remains one of fashion’s most enduring muses: not because she was styled into an icon, but because she became one on her own.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on June 03, 2026. May be an image of magazine and text that says 'This Editorial Changed Britneys Image Forever CULT®'.

posts.caption:
While punk became a fashion trend in many parts of the world, in Indonesia it never really stopped being a way of life.
Home to one of the largest punk communities on the planet, Indonesia’s punk scene has spent decades building its own identity, far away from the runways and trend cycles that later borrowed its aesthetic. From Jakarta to Bandung and Yogyakarta, punk became a voice for young people looking for community, independence, and a way to challenge social norms.
What makes the Indonesian scene so fascinating is its DIY spirit. Leather jackets covered in studs, hand-painted patches, towering mohawks, and custom battle vests aren’t costumes, they’re personal statements. Every jacket tells a story, every patch represents a band, a belief, or a friendship.
Despite facing social stigma and even government crackdowns over the years, the scene has remained remarkably resilient. Punk communities have organized concerts, art spaces, mutual aid projects, and underground networks that continue to thrive today.
The images might look like they belong to London in 1977, but they were taken thousands of miles away. They are a reminder that punk was never just a genre of music, it was a global movement. And while many scenes faded or became commercialized, Indonesia kept the spirit alive.
Sometimes the most authentic punk scenes are found in the places nobody expected.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on June 03, 2026. May be an image of magazine and text that says 'This Editorial Changed Britneys Image Forever CULT®'.

posts.caption:
Fashion has spent the last twenty years trying to recreate what Carolyn Bessette-Kennedy did naturally.
There was nothing complicated about the way she dressed. No obvious trends, no attention-grabbing styling tricks, no need to constantly reinvent herself. Yet somehow, a white shirt, a black skirt or a simple slip dress looked more elegant on her than most red carpet looks ever could.
That’s probably why people are still obsessed with her. Her outfits felt real. They weren’t built for social media, they weren’t chasing relevance, and they never looked overthought. She simply understood what worked for her and stuck to it.
More than two decades later, fashion still keeps returning to the same formula: clean silhouettes, understated luxury and effortless confidence. In many ways, we’re all still chasing the kind of elegance Carolyn made look so easy.
All images courtesy of Getty Images.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on June 03, 2026. May be an image of magazine and text that says 'This Editorial Changed Britneys Image Forever CULT®'.

For the Action Issue of @dazed, the racing prodigy reflects on influence, identity and the grounding presence of family – from early memories of the sport to Puja with his nani before races.

posts.caption:
Selkie just released imagery for a new collection inspired by the American flag.
Fans of the brand are confused; How could Selkie—which has always been a vehicle for LGBTQ+ voices, plus-size advocacy, diversity, and women’s liberation—put out a patriotic line during a time when patriotism is impossible to untangle from fascism?
To Selkie, that was the intention.
“We refuse to surrender the symbols of this country to those who would use them against us,” reads the Instagram caption below a carousel of women in ruffled, patinated stars-and-stripes dresses.
That is, Selkie wants to reclaim the flag. In their posts, they cite how suffragettes reclaimed stars-and-stripes by sewing customized flags celebrating states where women could vote. This is a tough sell; The flag is difficult to ‘reclaim’ if it was historically wielded against you, as in the case of slaves uprooted from their ancestral countries or native tribes whose country was uprooted from beneath their feet.
There have been instances throughout fashion history when oppressive, colonialist flags were reclaimed for the sake of rebellion. The Union Jack, for example, was used by punks in the 70s to criticize the value systems of the establishment. Vivienne Westwood’s ‘God Save The Queen’ t-shirts quoted Sex Pistols lyrics to represent London punk culture during Queen Elizabeth’s “Silver Jubilee” celebration. The look was imbued with irony and featured the queen’s face over lyrics like “there is no future in England’s dream.”
Does the design of Selkie’s collection inspire rebellion? For many viewers, it appropriates antebellum style without necessarily calling it to question. It also brings to mind Elena Velez’s controversial ‘Gone With the Wind’ AW24 show which had many of the same underpinnings, but with an alt-right twist. Because of this misstep, fans are beginning to view the entire Selkie image in a new light. That is to say that they’re connecting violence with American nostalgism in ways that Selkie, a purveyor of 18th-19th century prairie dresses, formerly circumvented.
Can Selkie reclaim the flag for the girls and the gays?

posts.caption:
Choupette has yet to receive her $1.5 million inheritance from her former owner, Karl Lagerfeld, who died in 2019.
Following Lagerfeld’s death, the blue-cream Birman was left in the care of Lagerfeld’s housekeeper, Françoise Caçote, who lives in a Paris apartment with her family and another cat who is a rescue. The money left to Choupette would help Caçote support Choupette, but French law prohibits animals from inheriting. Caçote said she is “doing her best” to honor Lagerfeld’s wishes.
Mr. Lagerfeld’s wishes can’t be easy to honor. In life, his love for Choupette was notorious, even going so far as to suggest he would marry Choupette if it were legal. Lagerfeld’s icy demeanor was melted by Choupette’s enormous blue eyes, which were the inspiration for a Shu Uemura makeup line and a Chanel collection. He described the cat as “the center of the world.”
But, Choupette was not just the beloved pet of a legendary designer. She was a working model in her own right. It has been reported that Choupette made more than $3 million as a model in 2014 alone, putting her in league with top models of that year including Cara Delevingne and Kendall Jenner. Choupette flew on private jets and cavorted with the world’s biggest celebrities. She appeared on ‘The Kardashians’ in 2023, hissing and clawing at Kim Kardashian who had hoped to take her as a date to the Met Gala.
Among Choupette’s many lavish possessions (she had two hired caretakers and her own iPad) her favorites were most likely her custom Goyard feeding bowls. Sotheby’s described the bowls as “a silver-plated dish with a beaded rim and a rupee representing Queen Victoria in profile” alongside “a second one with a gilt inside” and a third “resting on a tripod base, the interior gilt and engraved CHOUPETTE, topped by a parrot,” all of which sat upon matching silver trays. The pieces were auctioned off as a set for between €300 and €400.
Choupette’s fall from grace is an undeniable tragedy to everyone who knew her in her prime. Will justice be served for her? Or is she doomed to a life as a fallen regency?

posts.caption:
As AI continues to seep into fashion, some brands are leaning on the tech to create ads, including the models in them. Last year, H&M created “digital twins” of thirty models for a campaign (boooo). Similarly, Nike used AI for a campaign featuring Serena Williams playing a tennis match against herself (boooo again). Now, a lawsuit is questioning the ethics of AI-generated models, this time involving fast-fashion retailer Rainbow Shops.
NYC–based model Francheska Pujols alleges the company hired her for a catalog shoot, then used those photos to generate entirely new images, placing her in different settings, changing her poses, and even adding other models, all without her consent.
One image highlighted in the complaint, filed May 22 in New York Supreme Court, shows that an image of Pujols standing in front of a white backdrop was altered to make the model straddle a barstool. She called the image “crude,” saying it damaged her reputation.
According to the contract which reportedly expired in March, Rainbow Shops was only allowed  to make minor edits, not create new images.
Pujols claims she sent a cease-and-desist to the brand, which was allegedly ignored, and that the images continued to circulate on the company’s website as well as in online and in-store ads.
She has since dropped the suit. Her lawyer said in a statement that the parties are “seeking to resolve this matter privately.”
Last June, advocacy nonprofit Model Alliance helped pass the Fashion Workers Act in NY, which includes protections against AI use for scenarios exactly like this. Seems like Rainbow didn’t care until Pejols got lawyered up, funny how that works.
It’s bad enough AI is stealing land and water, now we have to worry about them stealing our likenesses? Really hoping more states adopt similar protections for models, this is ridiculous!
#model #fashion #shop #style #news

posts.caption:
Euphoria’s final episode aired last night. Now that it’s over, it’s worth looking back and considering what a massive impact it had in fashion.
The show debuted in 2019 and was explosively polarizing. But what held the dicey material together was its distinct attention to styling, makeup art, and Tumblr-y production design that oscillated between hazy, grainy environments and hallucinogenic visuals. Much of the visual language can be credited to the photography of Petra Collins who worked on the show as the director before being fired by HBO.
Hunter Schafer’s character Jules was a particular touchpoint. Her appearance on television coincided with increased visibility on trans issues, particularly for teens. Jules provided a face to scores of young trans people who embraced ravey, coquetteish, anime-inspired aesthetics. Viral 2020s trends like the Lirika Hitoshi strawberry dress seem connected to Jule’s influence.
Alexa Demie’s Maddy had a fast-fashion, Revolve-esque style that prioritized sex-appeal for the male gaze without compromising her ‘slay’ for the female gaze; an ethos that seems especially potent to a generation who grew up on Instagram. Her BFF Kat, played by Barbie Ferreira, offered rare plus-size representation and a Y2K alt-girl flavor that was both the result of and catalyst for a push toward size-inclusivity.
Before Euphoria, the style of the late 2010s gave minimalism and athleisure. Going into 2020, the show gave fans permission to experiment with creative, individualized makeup and bold styling. TikTok fueled hyper trends (This-core! That-core!) appeared. The first seeds of the Indie Sleaze revival were planted, and the more laidback crowd followed Rue’s cool tomboy lead in oversized tees and cargo shorts à la Billie Eilish.
Now nearly every Euphoria actor has become a veritable style icon in real life. And, onscreen, the final season featured who its troubled teens grew up to become: Young adults who never tone down their style for anyone—because they’ve grown up in a horrifically tumultuous sociopolitical landscape, so why NOT go all out?—but who, by contrast, will never let go of their favorite old hoodie when they need comfort.

posts.caption: “Things have never been more equal” okay so before men were doing ZERO household chores and now they are doing some…does not make it equal. Women who date men are still guaranteed to do MORE hours of household chores than if they live alone, while earning less dollars per hour. Until we address the very real income gaps, the downplaying of invisible domestic labor, and the fragility of subscription to the patriarchy, it will not be “great” to be straight

posts.caption:
For years, India has been known as the world’s jewellery manufacturing powerhouse.
Now it’s being recognised for something else: design. 🇮🇳✨
At the GJEPC India Design Gallery during JCK Las Vegas, a new generation of Indian designers showcased bold creativity, fresh perspectives and exceptional craftsmanship on one of the industry’s biggest global stages.
From intricate artistry to contemporary design thinking, these collections were a reminder that India’s future isn’t just about making jewellery.
It’s about shaping it.
What’s the first word that comes to mind when you think of Indian jewellery design?
credits - @gjepcindia @jckevents
👇 One word only.
#JCK2026 #IndianJewellery #DiamondWorld #GJEPC #JewelleryDesign
posts.alt_text:
The future of diamond jewellery isn’t round. It’s creative.

Ankit Shah of Ankit Gems highlights how fancy-shaped diamonds are giving designers something every retailer wants: differentiation.

While round diamonds remain the classic choice, shapes like pear, oval, cushion, marquise, princess, and emerald cuts unlock new possibilities in design, storytelling, and customer appeal.

💡 The biggest advantage?
Fancy shapes help retailers offer something customers haven’t seen before.

In mature markets like the US, Europe, and the Middle East, consumers moved beyond rounds in search of individuality. That same shift is now creating opportunities for retailers looking to stand out.

Because in today’s market, uniqueness isn’t a trend — it’s a competitive advantage.

💬 Retailers — are your customers asking for classic diamonds, or something different?

#leadersofindustry #DiamondWorld #IndianJeweller #DiamondTrends #RetailInnovation

posts.caption:
💛 Yellow diamonds are having a serious moment in Vegas.
From subtle accents to statement centre stones, yellow diamonds are appearing across some of the most talked-about collections at JCK 2026. Bold, vibrant and impossible to miss, they’re emerging as one of the strongest trends we’ve spotted on the Vegas show floor.
What started as a niche category is beginning to look a lot more mainstream.
Trend or future staple?
Credits - @jckevents
👇 Tell us below.
#YellowDiamonds #JCK2026 #DiamondWorld #NaturalDiamonds #DiamondTrends

Trend Spotting at JCK 2026 ⌚👀
One of the most unexpected trends emerging at JCK 2026 is the rise of jewellery-inspired timepieces and watch rings.
Blending nostalgia, collectibility and fashion, these miniature timekeepers are proving that consumers are looking for pieces that are as conversation-worthy as they are wearable.
Would you wear a watch ring?

posts.caption:
🎙️ Diamond World Exclusive | JCK Las Vegas 2026
In an exclusive conversation with Diamond World, Sandeep Shah, President of Sandeep Diamond Corporation USA, reflects on a remarkable 40-year journey that began with a simple mission: building a diamond business in America.
From loose diamonds to bridal jewellery, cutting-edge innovation and partnerships with some of the finest retailers across the country, the company has continued to evolve with the industry.
But according to Shah, the foundation of long-term success isn’t product.
It’s relationships.
Four decades later, that’s the lesson that still matters most. 💎
👇 In today’s jewellery industry, what’s harder to build?
Trust or Business?
#DiamondWorld #JCK2026 #DiamondBusiness #NaturalDiamonds #JewelleryIndustry

posts.caption:
For years, yellow diamonds were often viewed as the “accessible” side of fancy colour diamonds.
That may be exactly why they’re becoming so interesting.
While blue diamonds dominate headlines and pink diamonds command extreme rarity premiums, yellow diamonds occupy a unique space: they combine collectibility, rarity and broader commercial appeal.
From Blue Ivy Carter’s yellow diamond Met Gala moment to the growing spotlight on colour at JCK 2026, the signals are becoming harder to ignore.
💛 The biggest shift isn’t happening in colour.
It’s happening in consumer mindset.
Luxury buyers increasingly want pieces that feel personal, distinctive and difficult to replicate.
And yellow diamonds are quietly benefiting from that change.
#DiamondPulse #YellowDiamonds #FancyColorDiamonds #LuxuryRetail #DiamondWorld

posts.caption:
You can’t wear this jewellery.
Yet it contains 26,649 gemstones, 37.5 pounds of gold, and more than 106,000 prongs.
Commissioned by royalty and crafted over 16,000 hours, The Royal Tapestry remains one of the greatest engineering achievements in jewellery history.
Sometimes the most extraordinary jewellery was never meant to be worn.
What’s more impressive: the gemstones, the gold, or the engineering?
#DiamondWorld #BeyondJewellery #HighJewellery #LuxuryCollectibles #JewelleryDesign

posts.caption:
Satya Paul just unveiled its first collection under Aseem Kapoor’s creative direction on the grand staircase of Lancaster House in London. Yes, THAT Lancaster House. The one from The Crown. Think of this as a “teaser trailer” for the larger Satya Paul overhaul currently underway.
The prints, once largely confined to florals, have now been swapped for archival excavations that feel far more dangerous. Exotic polka-dotted snakes, tiger and zebra slashes, fish-scale motifs, and an archival peacock-feather print drifting across delicate silks and chiffons.
The presentation also had menswear, and under Aseem it meant tailored separates layered over flowy drapes and the same print overload as the women’s line. Fabrics were pulled, twisted, and gathered in every possible direction, while saris and scarves confetti-ed across the body, styled by Nikhil D. Headbands, capes, and kaftans gave the whole thing a louche, vintage-Italian-Pucci-by-way-of-South Delhi energy.
The Satya Paul woman is no longer an ingénue. She’s now a country-club girlie whose husband has gone suspiciously missing somewhere near Lake Como hehe.
I spoke to a bunch of women after the show who said they’d wear everything straight off the runway. A true desirability marker if you may. Still need to see more of Aseem’s long-term vision for the house, but the appetite is clearly there. That’s all!

posts.caption:
Remember Anisha? “Meri agency, meri reputation” etc? What a role. @iamkitugidwani bodied it!!
Because of the advent of A.I. and everything slowly flattening into code, prompts, algorithms, dashboards, and optimisation-slop, culture is weirdly circling back to the oldest currency of all: taste. Actual taste. Scary! The intangible thing you cannot automate no matter how many Midjourney or Claude prompts you generate. Which is why the women in branding, fashion, media, luxury, beauty, production etc, all the so-called “marketing girlies”, are emerging as the final arbiters of culture. The gatekeepers of what’s in, what becomes aspirational, what becomes cringe, what enters the group chat, and more.
Brands now shape culture more than institutions do, and the people shaping those brands inevitably become cultural architects. Meanwhile the finance bros, growth hackers, optimisation kings, LinkedIn hustle philosophers lol, and low-octave voice-using male podcasters have decided they too are now “creative directors” because they discovered the words storytelling and community in a PPM deck.
So girlies, are y’all reclaiming the “marketing girly” aka women in SPAM or no?

I understand that reality television is heavily scripted and exaggerated for shock value, but this series pushed it into a territory so relentlessly misogynistic that I genuinely had to stop midway. Every time the husbands spoke, I felt like throwing something at the TV.

Satya Paul just unveiled its first collection under Aseem Kapoor’s creative direction on the grand staircase of Lancaster House in London. Yes, THAT Lancaster House. The one from The Crown. Think of this as a “teaser trailer” for the larger Satya Paul overhaul currently underway.

The prints, once largely confined to florals, have now been swapped for archival excavations that feel far more dangerous. Exotic polka-dotted snakes, tiger and zebra slashes, fish-scale motifs, and an archival peacock-feather print drifting across delicate silks and chiffons.

The presentation also had menswear, and under Aseem it meant tailored separates layered over flowy drapes and the same print overload as the women’s line. Fabrics were pulled, twisted, and gathered in every possible direction, while saris and scarves confetti-ed across the body, styled by Nikhil D. Headbands, capes, and kaftans gave the whole thing a louche, vintage-Italian-Pucci-by-way-of-South Delhi energy.

The Satya Paul woman is no longer an ingénue. She’s now a country-club girlie whose husband has gone suspiciously missing somewhere near Lake Como hehe.

I spoke to a bunch of women after the show who said they’d wear everything straight off the runway. A true desirability marker if you may. Still need to see more of Aseem’s long-term vision for the house, but the appetite is clearly there. That’s all!

posts.caption:
People wear gowns. People wear tradition.
People wear elegance. But then Aishwarya Rai walks in, and suddenly fashion stops being fabric and becomes a moment in history. Time slows down. Eyes stay.
Conversations fade. Because some women don't follow beauty they become its definition. Years will pass, Cannes will see countless stars, but some queens aren't remembered for a look; they become part of the legacy itself. And Aishwarya Rai remains that legacy. ♥️🙏🏼

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: There is a version of Zinnia Kumar (now Rose) (@zinniakumar) that fashion understands easily. The model. An image that moves seamlessly between Paris, New York, and wherever the industry gathers next.
It is also the least interesting version of her.
We’re at Le Bristol in Paris and the day carries all the familiar markers of a luxury cover shoot. Controlled light. Precise framing. Cartier anchoring the narrative. But Rose doesn’t perform that script in the expected way. She arrives ready, slips into each look without negotiation, barely checks the monitor. There is no sense of overworking the image, only an instinctive understanding of how to move within it. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.
On @zinniakumar: Clash de Cartier earrings in rose gold and green dyed agate, Clash de Cartier necklace in rose gold, Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and chalcedony, Clash De Cartier ring in rose gold and onyx, and Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and red dyed agate, all by @cartier. Textured draped top paired and draped trousers both by @newbottega

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: Between takes, Zinnia Rose (@zinniakumar) speaks about working with domestic violence and coercive control survivors, particularly within South Asian communities. About helping women reclaim confidence, voice, and agency within legal systems that often fail to recognise the complexity of their experiences.
“I haven’t spoken about this publicly before, but I spent ten years in an abusive, financially and coercively controlling marriage,” she shares. “It shaped how much of myself I could express. Reclaiming myself and healing has been a long process, and one of the first things I did was remove the name he gave me. I’m returning to my birthright, as Zinnia Jothi Rose.”
Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.
On @zinniakumar: Clash de Cartier earrings in rose gold and onyx, Clash de Cartier necklace in yellow gold and onyx, Clash de Cartier ring in yellow gold and onyx, and Clash de Cartier bracelet in yellow gold and onyx, all by @cartier. #ELLECulture: Friendship breakups suck. For queer people a loss of a friend is not just that however- its a loss of a family member- the severance hurts twice as. And as does happen with friend groups, usually a friend breakup results in a split- but for us it's akin to a divorce. The result? Queer people sometimes being twice as skeptical as a regular joe when it comes to friendships. Head to the 🔗 in bio to understand why for queer people, a friendship breakup can feel less like losing a friend and more like losing family.
Words by : @judelogoutplease

posts.caption:
Presenting our first of four Summer Issue cover stars, @annehathaway.
Due to filming and release delays, Hathaway now has five movies premiering within six months. Her insanely busy year continues with her starring role as Penelope in @theodysseymovie. (To which she says: “It’s crazy to have four years of work coming out in six months.”) After weathering the storms of Hollywood, the in-demand star is finally collecting her flowers—and stopping to smell them. “If I’m not enjoying it, then really, really what’s wrong with me?” she tells ELLE. “If I can’t have a positive outlook on it when I’m having such a fortunate ride, then it would be wasted on me.”
For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, Hathaway reflects on how she’s grown since the early days of her career, reuniting with Christopher Nolan after 12 years, and navigating online criticism: “We’re at a time when people feel very confident in assuming what they think is fact.” Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
‘Widow’s Bay’ is (rightfully) being called one of the best shows of 2026—and Kate O’Flynn has already earned MVP status.
Thanks to her signature mix of frustration and defiance in approaching the nightmare scenarios plaguing the town, her character, Patricia, goes from being overlooked and disregarded to becoming an integral part of the show’s season one finale. “It’s so thrilling that something that I think is great—and I would have loved it if I wasn’t in it—is getting the response it is,” she tells ELLE.
We caught up with the breakout star from her home in London to talk her audition for the hit series, working with co-star Matthew Rhys, and why she found the season one finale to be  “weirdly moving” and “really profound.” Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
@authorbkborison has always known she would write Maggie and Cooper's love story in the third installment of her bestselling ‘Heartstrings’ series; she also understood it would be a daunting task.
‘Longtime Listener,‘ the supposedly final book in the series, is drawn from the plot of Nora Ephron's rom-com classic ‘You've Got Mail,’ continuing the tradition of loosely basing the other two installments on the iconic writer-director's  works. Here, radio station manager Maggie and cunning businessman Cooper mirror Meg Ryan and Tom Hanks's onscreen characters, starting as two opposing forces who—somehow!—come together to have a romantic relationship. Of the three books, Borison says this one feels ”the most rom-com-y.”
“When I tell people what the third one is based on, there’s a gasp around the room, because people love it so much,” Borison says. “That’s the challenge with being inspired by classics, these films that have transcended time and everybody adores: You take on a lot when you tell people, ‘Oh, it’s like You’ve Got Mail.’ I think I’ve intimidated myself a little bit in the process.”
Ahead of Borison's return next year with ‘Longtime Listener,’ the bestelling romance author gives ELLE an exclusive first look at the new installment and tells us what to expect. Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
@sonyayu wants to bring art to the people.
At the start of 2026, @momaps1 became the largest free museum in New York City, thanks to a $900,000 donation from Yu. A rising force in the art world, the entrepreneur, art collector, and founder of the creative agency Four One Nine has covered admission for all visitors for three years. It’s a big win not only for the institution, which is celebrating its 50th anniversary this year, but also for the public, as museum ticket prices rise across the globe. “I want to be able to provide access in this specific, personalized way to a community that I could really make an impact on,” Yu tells ELLE’s @ericagonzo.
Though she lives roughly 3,000 miles away from PS1, its surrounding neighborhood resonated with her: It’s home to many Asian and Hispanic residents—immigrants like Yu, who moved to the U.S. from China when she was 5 years old. Free admission, she says, could give locals and tourists of all backgrounds “the opportunity to go to the museum and not do the daily math that we are burdened with.”
Read ELLE’s full interview with Yu at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
For her most whimsical high jewelry collection yet, Dior Joaillerie artistic director @victoiredecastellane turned to symbols of joy. Unveiled in Venice during the Biennale, “Diorissima” is a vibrant celebration of suns, stars, flowers, sea creatures, and dreamlike landscapes rendered in extraordinary gemstones and colorful lacquer. The collection’s first chapter features 112 creations, from transformable brooches to striking necklaces and rings, with standouts including the floral-inspired Récif Fleuri brooch and the Galon Fleuri ring, anchored by an almost 16-carat black opal.
At the link in bio, ELLE’s executive fashion and accessories director @awolfestyle takes a closer look at the collection.

posts.caption:
The ink pen may be one of the most nostalgic casualties of the digital age. Once a symbol of discipline, aspiration and careful craftsmanship, it has largely disappeared from classrooms, offices and everyday life. Yet mention an ink pen, and stories come rushing back—of stained white shirts, leaky school bags, handwriting drills and the rituals that came with putting pen to paper.
Head to the link in bio to read how a fountain pen unlocked a flood of forgotten memories.
Written by: Rudra Mulmule (@poetryandpasta)
#EsquireIndia #InkPens

posts.caption: My outfit definitely expresses how I’m feeling. I might get extra done up if I’m feeling bad or in a funk and I don’t feel good when I’m not put together. But there are many people that just get dressed and don’t think about it one way or another so I’m not sure that it affects everyone in the same way.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover

posts.caption:
On 28 May, the European Commission fined Temu under the Digital Services Act — the second-largest DSA penalty ever issued, after X got hit for €120 million last year.
The headline is dangerous products: baby toys with chemicals over safety limits, chargers that failed basic tests. But look at what the Commission’s mystery shopping exercise also found: clothing made with banned chemicals. Temu is one of the EU’s most-used fashion platforms. This is a fashion story.

For over six decades, Femina Miss India has been more than a pageant. It’s woven into the cultural fabric of India. It is a platform that has consistently celebrated ambition, individuality, resilience, and purpose, highlighting the inherent beauty of our women on the global stage.
This year’s winners are Femina Miss India World 2026 Sadhvi Sail, Femina Miss India 2026 – 1st Runner Up Rajnandini Pawar, and Femina Miss India 2026 – 2nd Runner Up Dr Sree Advaita, each representing modern Indian women. Together, they embody the spirit of Femina Miss India: connected by purpose, united by ambition, and ready to shape the future on their own terms.

#FeminaWellness: Between the last job and the next opportunity lies a chapter we don’t talk about enough: funemployment.
A season of slower mornings, spontaneous plans, unfinished hobbies finally getting their moment, and the freedom to spend your time exactly how you choose. It’s a chance to step away from the constant rush, invest in yourself, explore new interests, and enjoy life beyond your job title. If you’re currently funemployed, don’t just count the days, make them count!
#Femina #Wellness #Funemployment #PersonalGrowth

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 21, 2026.

posts.caption:
Meet the top rated brands of 2025 🎊⁠
⁠
In the past year, we rated more than 1500 brands and are proud to share the ten that received our highest marks, illustrating a commitment to more sustainable practices that protect people, the planet, and animals 💚⁠
⁠
What’s a theme that unites these brands? 👀 Outside of a commitment to reducing their impact, these brands are all small and independent, a reminder of where to look in the industry for leadership and innovation. If you’re looking for a reminder to support small brands this year, this is it.⁠
⁠
Cheers to all these brands, along with all the brands that were rated “Good” and “Great” in the past year! Here’s to continuing to make a difference and for championing a better approach to what we wear. Find the full list at the link—and share which brands you’re hoping to see rated in 2026 in the comments 💬⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 21, 2026.

posts.caption:
What can be accomplished in ten years?⁠
⁠
A decade may seem short but, in reflecting on all the work we’ve done in the past decade, we’re in awe of everything that has transpired—and are grateful for the hard work of the current and former team members along with you.⁠
⁠
Whether you’ve shared our posts or have been a partner in this business, the work of creating a more sustainable future isn’t done alone but in partnership with others. These accomplishments are an example of what happens when likeminded people come together in the hopes of a better world 🌍🌎🌏⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn about ten things Good On You has accomplished in the past ten years. Share how you think the sustainable fashion and beauty industry have shifted in the past decade 💚⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion

posts.caption:
More brands need to care about the planet 🌏🌎🌍⁠
⁠
That’s why environmental impact is one of three key points considered in our rating, which includes everything from circular practices to textile waste, pollution to product durability. When clothes are made with the planet in mind, its impact is *greatly* reduced 💚⁠
⁠
Not all brands are taking these measures of care seriously, as our experts have found in reviewing over 7000 brands. So…which brands are doing the best for the world?⁠
⁠
From @outlanddenim to @bastetnoir, swipe to meet them 💫 Visit the link to learn more and discover more brands that care for the planet ↑⁠

Just because you’re going to a music festival doesn’t mean you leave your values at home 🪶⁠
⁠
For years, fast fashion—and most big brands—have been pushing themselves into the conversation of festivals, monopolizing attention in the hopes that you buy, buy, buy what they’re selling. Don’t fall for it ⛔⁠
⁠
Instead, use this as an opportunity to flex your commitment to sustainability, by sharing clothes and wearing what you have. In need of something specific? This is where “Good” and “Great” brands come in, which are guaranteed to have better values while typically being small and independent.⁠

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Matt Damon as Odysseus.
A Hollywood veteran of nearly five decades now, Damon has worked on approximately 80 film sets. And yet, as he told GQ’s @zachbaron, “Every single location on this movie would’ve been the hardest location on any other movie I’ve ever done… Every time we’d go somewhere, we’d be like, ‘Well, Iceland will be easier.’ And then it’s raining sideways and it’s fucking freezing. Iceland was like, ‘Yeah, easy? Hey, hold my beer.’”
For Damon, though, the challenge was part of what made the project so special: “All of the things that might have been difficult for me at an earlier point in my life weren’t difficult. They became fun. Like being wet and cold was not a hardship. It was actually something I felt really lucky to be experiencing. It felt temporary and like a gift.”
Damon unwraps the ‘gift’ that was making The Odyssey, alongside his co-stars Robert Pattinson and Tom Holland, and director Christopher Nolan, for GQ’s summer issue cover story at the 🔗 in bio.

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Robert Pattinson as Antinous.
Pattinson’s part in The Odyssey is not big—he plays the cockiest of the many suitors who have installed themselves at Ithaca, seeking the hand of Penelope (played by Anne Hathaway) while Matt Damon’s Odysseus is away. “My character’s a little like James Woods in Casino. That was my inspiration for it,” Pattinson said. “He’s like a little sleazy. I kept saying at my costume fitting, ‘I really want to have leopard underpants.’”
Pattinson talks to GQ’s @zachbaron about teaming up with Nolan again for The Odyssey, alongside co-stars Matt Damon and Tom Holland, at the 🔗 in bio.

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Tom Holland as Telemachus.
When Christopher Nolan first offered Holland a part in The Odyssey, the production dates were identical to those of Spider-Man: Brand New Day (and Holland’s commitments to the Spider-Man franchise are ironclad). “I said to Chris, like, ‘Look, I want to do this movie, but if I’m going to do it, I’m going to have to call Sony and have a very uncomfortable conversation,’ ” Holland said. Then he immediately had a very uncomfortable conversation.
“Chris has that reputation of ‘This movie isn’t going to go five months over, and we aren’t actually going to lose Tom for two years,’ ” Holland said. “Any other director, it might have been a slightly different conversation.” The Odyssey started on the day Nolan said it would and finished nine days early.
For our summer cover story, Holland talks about coming of age on set, getting to star alongside his partner on another project, and how he’s approaching the future of his career at the 🔗 in bio.

posts.caption:
Christopher Nolan is a paradoxical figure. His films, despite their size and expense, often deal with knotty ideas about time and space, and are not always fully understood, even by his own actors.
But it’s not hard to see the march forward through the human experience in his films, including his latest, The Odyssey: “Whenever I got married, I was making films about the motivating force being a guy’s wife who died or whatever. And then I had kids and started making films about kids.”
Matt Damon, who plays the films titular character and has four children, recalls reading Nolan’s Interstellar for the first time on a movie set: “I was so emotional reading this script because I had little kids. I was overseas, away from them for two weeks, but I was still away from them. And that script is about losing the entire childhood of your kid. And I remember calling him after and going like, ‘What the fuck is it with you about this?’”
At the 🔗 in bio, read the story of how @theodysseymovie stars Matt Damon, Robert Pattinson, and Tom Holland led a cast and crew of hundreds to help Christopher Nolan create his most epic and ambitious blockbuster to date.
Story by @zachbaron

posts.caption:
There is nothing else that currently exists in filmmaking like a Christopher Nolan production.
“If you’re lucky enough to succeed with a project,” the director explains, “you kind of have a responsibility as a filmmaker to try and say, ‘Okay, what can I do? What am I allowed to do now that maybe I wouldn’t have been allowed to do before or somebody else wouldn’t be allowed to do?’”
“Obviously, coming off the back of the success of Oppenheimer, it was like, All right, we’ve got to use this opportunity to try and really do the most ambitious thing that we can.” Nolan soon decided the most ambitious thing he could do was: adapt Homer’s Odyssey.
At the 🔗 in bio, read GQ’s Summer cover story on how Hollywood’s three leading-est men Matt Damon, Robert Pattinson, and Tom Holland led a cast and crew of hundreds to help Nolan create his most epic and ambitious blockbuster to date.

posts.caption:
We’ll take careers advice from Michelle Obama any day.
The former First Lady was speaking at a live podcast recording at the SXSW London festival yesterday alongside her brother, and podcast co-host Craig Robinson, about her early career experiences. ‘One thing that’s important is to learn how to do something you don’t like to do and be good at it… Not giving you the raise when you think you deserve it, there’s character building that gets you ready down the line before being your own manager, being your own leader.’
Her quotes might have been aimed at Gen Zs just starting out, but we’ll take the timely reminder from one of the wisest women in America, that professional setbacks and tricky moments are how we grow.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Grazia UK on June 03, 2026. May be an image of one or more people and text that says ''Every experience, the bad boss, the boring assistant job all of that is learning to be resilient.' MICHELLE BAMA GRAZIA'.

posts.caption:
The Princess of Wales knows how to wear her heart on her sleeve!
At a reception to mark the 125th anniversary of Cancer Research UK, Kate opted for her unique style of method dressing. Zoom in to the vintage inspired Rodarte tea-dress she was wearing and you’ll see mini hearts all over it.
Given the personal connection Kate has to the charity, having gone through her own cancer journey, perhaps she chose this to show her love for everyone battling the disease. And we love her for that.

posts.caption: Meet @rainaopr, your new favorite Puerto Rican artist. The singer, born Naomi Ramírez Rivera, first gained attention with her 2022 EP "ahora A.K.A. Nao," which led to performances alongside @BadBunnyPR in Puerto Rico and a collaboration on “PERFuMITO NUEVO.” Now, with her latest album, "Marcriá," she's stepping fully into her own. A sensual exploration of island life, the record balances nostalgia with the realities of everyday struggle—all while showcasing RaiNao's meticulous approach to composition. “With these arrangements I was very specific about how I wanted my voice to sound, what instruments I wanted to hear,” she says. Tap the link in bio to listen to the album and learn how it came together. Photo: @siempreric
