posts.caption:
Erling Braut Haaland has once again drawn attention for his luxury style after being spotted carrying a rare @hermes bag while traveling with Norway’s national team to the World Cup in the United States aboard a VIP charter flight.⁠
⁠
The Manchester City striker was seen with a Hermès HAC Birkin 50 “Endless Road” in Gris Perle, one of the French house’s most exclusive travel bags. Crafted in Togo leather with palladium-plated hardware, the model can fetch around $45,500 (approximately NOK 422,000) on the resale market. Originally designed to transport equestrian equipment, the HAC (Haut à Courroies) remains one of Hermès’ most coveted travel silhouettes, with larger sizes such as the HAC Birkin 50 produced in particularly limited quantities.⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Highsnobiety Style #HSStyle on June 03, 2026. May be an image of suit and text that says 'NOTHING THIS COLLECTION WAS SUPPOSED ΤΟ WORK 브'.

posts.caption:
Rather than starting with a mood board or a fixed concept, @stockbridge.us collection emerged through instinct, experimentation, and an obsession with material. @charlie.stockbridge spent more time discussing fabric swatches than the collection itself, gravitating toward unconventional Japanese knits, deadstock textiles, and fabrics that felt strange, unfamiliar, or simply interesting. Existing silhouettes were revisited and reworked, including the return of a self-referential bag first made by hand shortly after college, reflecting a broader process of refinement and reexamination across the brand.⁠
⁠
The accompanying imagery extends that same mindset. Inspired by the contrast between nature and the city, the campaign centers around a handcrafted bench built by the designer as both a tribute to his late grandfather, who worked in the Forest Service, and an exploration of woodworking, furniture design, and fabrication. Transported into the studio alongside dirt, greenery, and hiking-inspired objects, the bench serves as a reminder to pause, take in your surroundings, and embrace the unexpected. ⁠
⁠
Like the collection itself, the project was guided less by intention than by curiosity, allowing ideas to reveal themselves through the process rather than before it.⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Highsnobiety Style #HSStyle on June 03, 2026. May be an image of headscarf, magazine and text that says 'HAALAND JUST PULLED UP tO THE WORLD CUP WITH A RARE HERMES BAG e 브'.

posts.caption:
Football's biggest stage is getting the @corteiz treatment. ⁠
⁠
To celebrate its World Cup collection, the London label is taking the drop on the road with an 11-city tour, bringing a lineup of jerseys, tracksuits, caps, scarves, and more to football fans around the globe. Blending football culture, streetwear, and the spirit of the tournament, the collection turns match-day merch into wearable souvenirs.⁠
⁠
The first stop of the tour is Friday, May 6, with the other stops to be announced. Swipe for a closer look.

posts.caption:
Martin Margiela is having a proper clear-out. But for one of fashion’s most influential designers, that means parting with hundreds of archive pieces, original sketches, paper patterns, fabric samples, and design documents that helped shape Maison Margiela. On July 9, Maurice Auction will offer 276 lots dating from 1988 to 1994, marking what it describes as the first time a living creator has directly collaborated with an auction house to sell their personal archive.⁠
⁠
The sale spans far beyond collectible garments, though highlights include Margiela’s hand-painted Spring/Summer 1990 blazer and a Spring/Summer 1993 necklace crafted from cotton ribbon and a tree branch. Most revealing, however, are the behind-the-scenes materials: prototype logos, annotated design folders, production documents, original drawings of the iconic tabi boot, and paper patterns believed to date back to the brand’s earliest collections. Together, they offer the closest look yet inside Margiela’s creative process and the foundations of one of fashion’s most influential maisons.⁠

posts.caption:
Team Mexico wears Willy 🌹🇲🇽 As the World Cup nears, @adidasoriginals and @wiillychavarria are using their collaborative line to launch the “Comienza Con El Sueño” collection — a dedicated capsule for Selección Nacional de México.
Directly translating to “It Begins With the Dream,” the capsule channels deep-rooted Mexican football heritage through Chavarria’s distinct retro sportswear.
The expansive lineup reimagines on-pitch staples alongside coach-ready silhouettes, featuring FMF-badged soccer jerseys, tracksuits, tailored blazers, and long twill shorts. Footwear also takes things to the next level with the Willy Mega Low and Megaride AG XL, boasting exaggerated uppers and striking red accents, plus the football-inspired Megaride Copa.
The adidas Originals x Willy Chavarria collection officially drops globally on June 10 via adidas, CONFIRMED, and select retailers.

posts.caption:
There are people who wear time, and then there are people who define it. @palaktiwarii belongs to the second kind.✨
She carries something rare, a weight that isn’t borrowed from the past, but isn’t chasing the future either. It simply exists, with the kind of quiet confidence that doesn’t need to announce itself.
In this frame, legacy and the now collapse into each other. @guesswatches on her wrist, intent in her eyes, highlighting the “Main Character” hunger behind the gold hardware; this is a story of a woman who isn’t just wearing the accessories of success, but is actively forging them. ⌚️
The Architecture of Time isn’t a concept. It’s a posture. A way of owning every second without letting a single one own you.
@palaktiwarii doesn’t follow the tempo. She sets it. And right now, she’s setting it for all of us. 🫶🏻

posts.caption:
@jennifer.garner *always* knows what to do (and it usually involves sorghum).
Between starring in summer’s buzziest new series, taking her near-billion dollar company public, and turning ‘Pretend Cooking Show’ into a reality, she still manages to be the nicest woman in Hollywood.
Playing a widowed food blogger in @Peacock’s adaptation of @elinhilderbrand’s bestseller ‘The Five-Star Weekend,’ the beloved actress and entrepreneur channels her inner Ina Garten for a not-so-Instagrammable girls’ weekend on Nantucket with co-stars-turned-IRL besties, @chloesevigny, @morereginahall, @gemmachan and @darcycarden, exploring what it means to grow and develop in middle age.
At 54, the Marvel Hero and supermom reflects on her genre-spanning career, being “total besties” with John Cena, her surprisingly vast knowledge of weaponry, plus the responsibility of celebrities to stand up for working locally for InStyle’s Summer Imagemakers Issue at the link in @instylemagazine’s bio now.

posts.caption:
Ever taken in a big whiff of seaweed? I’d bet money that your first thought was not, Omg, how do I bottle this? A new wave of oceanic, salty scents is overtaking the typically tropical slate of warm-weather perfumes. Consider it an olfactory reset.
This summer’s take on beachy perfumes encapsulates the calming, pensive, and healing nature of the seashore. They aren’t loud and energetic. Instead, they’re refreshing and refined. For a summery scent that actually feels like a quick dip on a breezy day, spritz on one of these oceanic fragrances, and learn more about the tides of the nostalgic trend at the link in @instylemagazine’s bio. 🌊
✍️ @karajillianbrown

posts.caption:
After his dominant Western Conference Finals performance against the reigning champion, Wembanyama is arriving at the greatness the league expected of him, ahead of time. The 7’5 superstar is changing the texture of professional basketball itself: he’s restoring faith in it. Making it feel, again, like something worth watching. It’s like he’s saying, simply with his earnest to the game that: “Here is someone you can put your faith in, here is the next generation of basketball, here is the next face of the NBA.”
The NBA (@nba) Finals begin on June 4th in San Antonio with the Spurs (@spurs) led by Victor Wembanyama (@wemby) facing off against the New York Knicks (@nyknicks) and Jalen Brunson (@jalenbrunson1). The  most scrutinised NBA finals since GSW and the Cavaliers in 2018. The Knicks are in the finals for the first time in almost three decades, meanwhile Wemby is in the finals on his first playoff runs.
Chirrag Guha (@guha.archie) breaks down Victor Wembanyama’s meteoric rise at the link in bio ahead of the blockbuster matchup.
[ Victor Wembanyama, San Antonio Spurs, NBA Finals, New York Knicks, Kobe Bryant, Lebron James, Jalen Brunson]

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
«Ci sono persone davvero brave in determinate cose, e tutte insieme funzionano in modo brillante, ma non tutti possono essere eccellenti in tutto. Negli anni ho imparato a capire cosa ti motiva e in che modo puoi essere utile a un progetto, soprattutto quando lavori in collaborazione», dichiara @KateHudson nella sua intervista con L’OFFICIEL.
«Se metti insieme persone di cui ti fidi e che sono brave in quello che fanno, ottieni la migliore esperienza possibile. Quando un ambiente di lavoro è instabile o caotico, raramente porta al successo».

posts.caption:
180 anni, Madrid, 1846. @Loewe — la seconda maison di lusso più antica al mondo — celebra quasi due secoli di eccellenza con una campagna fotografata da Talia Chetrit, con Julia Garner Giselle, Salma Abu Deif, Kara Wai, Sissy Spacek e Kara Walker. Il motivo leonino — Loewe, in tedesco, significa leone — percorre l’intera collezione capsule disponibile dal 4 giugno. E per chi vuole andare ancora più in fondo: un film d’animazione con la voce di Antonio Banderas ripercorre i 180 anni della maison, e un supplemento speciale «180 Years of Craft» accompagnerà il numero 11 del LOEWE Magazine dal 15 giugno. La storia non invecchia. Si moltiplica.
Photo: Courtesy of @Loewe
#Loewe #LOfficiel #LOfficielItalia

posts.caption:
Ottant’anni fa, il #2Giugno 1946, gli italiani - e per la prima volta le italiane - scelsero alle urne la #Repubblica al posto della monarchia. A immortalare quel momento fu il fotoreporter Federico Patellani con la sua “Donna della Repubblica”: il volto luminoso e sorridente di una ragazza, diventato l’emblema del referendum. La sua identità restò a lungo ignota, finché un’inchiesta ne svelò il nome. Oggi quegli scatti sono celebrati nella mostra ”Donna Repubblica. I giorni del referendum”, al Museo Nazionale di Fotografia di Cinisello Balsamo, dal 30 maggio al 5 luglio 2026. Continua a leggere l’articolo a cura di @AlessandroViapiana_ al link in bio.
Photo: Archivio Federico Patellani/Courtesy Regione Lombardia, Museo Nazionale di Fotografia, Milano-Cinisello Balsamo

posts.caption:
@KateHudson has taken on a wide range of genres, but she’s still focused on pushing herself even further. When we asked Hudson what she still hopes to achieve in her career, she didn’t point to accolades—just a desire to keep pursuing roles that scare her, the same approach she brings to everything she does. ⁠
⁠
“I enter every experience like it’s new, like I don’t know how it’s going to unfold,” she explains. “I come in doing my part, which is understanding what makes me feel confident in the role and the character, but I do that so that when I’m there, I can be flexible, not rigid.” Read Hudson’s full interview at the link in our bio. ⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Ever since @sarah__pidgeon described the color of her @chanelofficial Métiers d’Art 2026 collection look as “...toothpaste...but super chic toothpaste,” mint green became the color of the season. Sure, icy blue was winter’s go-to shade, while chartreuse had its moment this spring, but summer belongs to mint green. Imagine a warm summer day spent with friends: sunglasses on, mint green ensembles from the latest Spring/Summer 2026 collections, and a refreshing mojito in hand. If that’s not the blueprint for a chic summer, what is?
Not only does mint green come in multiple shades, but it is also one of the most versatile colors of the season. @chanelofficial, @fendi, and @dior have all tapped into the trend, showcasing the refreshing hue across summer wardrobe staples ranging from lightweight separates and dresses to handbags and footwear. Equal parts crisp and chic, mint green is proving to be fashion’s favorite new neutral. Visit the link in our bio for more on the trend!
Photos @dualipa and Getty Images
#colortrends #dualipa #sarahpidgeon #summerfashion

posts.caption:
Just when it seemed like the cultural grip of ‘Brat’ might never loosen, @charli_xcx is already moving on. If ‘Brat’ was the soundtrack to a generation’s messy nights out, ‘Music, Fashion, Film’ looks poised to capture what happens after the party ends: a world where pop stars become cultural architects, and where the boundaries between music, style, and cinema continue to dissolve. The album cover teased ahead of its July 24 release brings that idea into focus, featuring three cultural heavyweights: #JohnCale, @theMarcJacobs, and #MartinScorsese.
It got us thinking about the creative partnerships that have shaped culture far beyond their respective industries. From #AudreyHepburn and Hubert de Givenchy—whose collaboration gave us some of cinema’s most enduring looks in ‘Breakfast at Tiffany’s’—to Luca Guadagnino and Jonathan Anderson, whose work on ‘Challengers’ and ‘Queer’ blurred the lines between costume design and fashion, these collaborations have helped define how we see, dress, and experience pop culture.
Photos @charli_xcx and Getty Images
#charlixcx #charlixcxmusic

posts.caption:
As ‘Moulin Rouge!’ celebrates its 25th anniversary this June, @bazluhrmann’s Parisian world of cabaret, doomed romance, and excess still feels as intoxicating as ever—none more so than Satine, played by @NicoleKidman. In our 2024 cover interview, Kidman reflected on the unique gift acting has given her: the chance to inhabit places, stories, and lives she’d never otherwise experience. “It’s imprinted on my psyche in a way that I can go back and dream about it and go, I was in that place and that was me,” she said, describing how those worlds remain with her long after filming ends.
“I was living in those mountains, or in that desert, or in a tent, or riding a camel, or hiking a mountain in Belfast… these are things no one else gets to do.” As the film milestone is marked, we’re revisiting our 2024 cover conversation with the Oscar winner—and one of her most unforgettable cinematic destinations. Head to the link in our bio.
Photography @matthewbrookesphoto
Styled by @jasonbolden
#moulinrougemovie #bazluhrmann #nicolekidman

posts.caption:
Since the first Pride march in New York City on June 28, 1970, one year after the Stonewall Uprising, the celebration of queer rights and liberation has become a global tradition. From Los Angeles to London, communities have gathered for decades to honor the contributions of LGBTQ+ individuals while remembering those lost to the HIV/AIDS crisis.⁠
⁠
Ahead of this month's festivities, take a look back at how the parade has evolved around the globe over the last 55 years. See more photos of Pride at the link in our bio. ⁠
⁠
Photos Getty Images⁠
⁠
#NYCPride #PrideMonth #PrideParade

posts.caption:
@Balenciaga dropped its Spring Summer 2026 preview collection in Mumbai last night, and the energy in the room felt different.
With Fatima Sana Sheikh, Bobby Deol, Mouni Roy, Sanya Malhotra, Malavika Mohanan, and Krithi Shetty in attendance, it felt less like a formal showcase and more like a real moment around fashion. The room was sharp, observant, and engaged.
Fatima Sana Shaikh said it best: “Balenciaga always has a chic approach and the brand is now heading towards it even more aggressively.” And that tone carried through the evening. Nothing felt loud for attention, yet everything commanded it.
What stood out was how people responded. There was curiosity, closer inspection, and genuine interest in the details. Some silhouettes felt familiar but more refined, while others brought a quiet sense of drama without trying too hard.
That is when a collection works.
Not when it demands attention, but when it earns it.
@balenciaga #Balenciaga

posts.caption:
Jacob & Co. continues to blur the line between watchmaking, sculpture, and fantasy with the Astronomia “Gates of Olympus.”
Priced at $1.4 million, the one-of-one creation draws inspiration from Greek mythology, featuring Zeus, Olympus, celestial movement, and a fully mechanical miniature world built directly into the timepiece itself.
Inside the 18K rose gold case sits a triple-axis tourbillon, over 9 carats of baguette diamonds, and an intricate Olympus scene designed as part of the movement.
More than a watch, the piece reflects how modern haute horology is increasingly moving toward collectible art and immersive storytelling.
@JacobAndCo #JacobAndCo #Luxurious #Collab

posts.caption:
To mark the 100th anniversary of Marilyn Monroe’s birth, Blancpain has unveiled the Ladybird Tribute, an exclusive collection inspired by the legendary actress’s own Blancpain watch.
The collection consists of just seven unique timepieces, each representing a letter from MARILYN and featuring a distinct colour palette inspired by Monroe’s life and legacy. The watches reinterpret the diamond-set Blancpain cocktail watch once owned by the Hollywood icon, which resurfaced at auction in 2016 before becoming part of Blancpain’s heritage collection.
Crafted in white gold and adorned with 85 diamonds, the Ladybird Tribute blends vintage elegance with modern watchmaking. More than a commemorative release, it celebrates the enduring connection between one of Hollywood’s most iconic figures and one of Switzerland’s oldest watchmakers.
Images: @blancpain1735

posts.caption:
In the middle of the Texas desert sits one of fashion’s most unexpected landmarks: Prada Marfa.
Created in 2005 by artists Elmgreen & Dragset, the permanent art installation was designed to resemble a Prada boutique, complete with real Prada shoes and handbags selected from the brand’s Fall/Winter 2005 collection. Unlike a traditional store, however, it was never meant to open its doors. Instead, the structure was conceived as a work of art that would naturally age and deteriorate over time.
What began as an artistic commentary on luxury, consumerism, and branding has since become a global cultural icon. Supported by Miuccia Prada herself, the installation has attracted fashion enthusiasts, artists, celebrities, and tourists from around the world, transforming a remote stretch of desert into one of the most photographed destinations in fashion.
More than two decades later, Prada Marfa remains a powerful example of how fashion can transcend retail and become part of contemporary art and cultural history.
#Luxurious

posts.caption:
Bugatti has entered the world of ultra-luxury home entertainment with the new C SEED Bugatti N1, a folding MicroLED television inspired by the marque’s $4.6 million Tourbillon hypercar. The sculptural display is available in 110-inch and 137-inch formats and transforms from a sleek furniture-like sideboard into a giant cinematic screen in just 45 seconds.
Drawing design inspiration from Bugatti’s iconic C-line and the Tourbillon’s philosophy of “Art, Forme, Technique,” the N1 features precision-engineered folding panels, 4K MicroLED technology, HDR10+ support, integrated high-end audio, and a 180-degree rotating screen. When not in use, the display disappears back into its architectural form, prioritising design as much as technology.
More than a television, the Bugatti N1 represents the growing trend of luxury automotive brands extending their design language beyond cars and into lifestyle, interiors, and collectible technology.
📸: @bugatti

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
There is a quiet power to Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth). One that doesn’t seek attention, yet holds it effortlessly.
For Manifest’s May–June cover, she steps into an imaginative world where temple jewellery meets contemporary silhouettes, blurring the lines between memory and modernity. She embodies a woman in transition, grounded, luminous, and yet constantly becoming.
“I’m not very good at describing myself,” she says, almost in passing.
Yet, in the same breath, her choices reveal a quiet clarity of intent. “I don’t want to isolate my character. I need to understand the whole world it exists in.”
Between restraint and reinvention, stillness and movement, she exists in that rare in-between. Not fully defined, not confined. Just on the cusp of a wider sky.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story from the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Satya Paul (@satyapaulindia) just took its legacy of print, drape, and artistic expression to a whole new level at SXSW London with Untamed, the brand’s first international showcase under the creative direction of Aseem Kapoor (@aseemkapoor_official).
And if there’s one thing we took away from the collection, it’s this: wedding guest dressing just got a mood refresh. Whether you’re a bridesmaid making an entrance, a groom’s squad member serving a look, or simply headed to a beachside cocktail soirée, the fluid silhouettes, prints and the effortless ease are exactly what we want in our summer ensemble rotation.
Marking the global debut of Satya Paul’s occasionwear, the showcase brought together fashion, movement, and live performance in a way that felt more like an experience than a runway show. Actor Danish Pandor (@danishpandor) walked the showcase, while acclaimed percussionist and composer Sarathy Korwar (@sarathykorwar) joined British Tamil movement artist Hashna Siva (@hashnasiva) for a specially commissioned performance that transformed the presentation into an experience that will take its own sweet time to sink in.
The show became an ultimate statement of a designer’s vision and an exuberant vision board we’re already saving for our next wedding-season entrance.

posts.caption:
Some love stories begin with a perfectly timed meet-cute. Kanchi Shah (@kanchishaa) and Kartik Jaishankar’s (@kartikjaishankar) began with choreography. Less than a year later, the couple landed in Jaisalmer with a clear vision: a wedding that felt like them. Not a performance of tradition, but an immersive celebration of the lives, families, and cultures that shaped them.
The two first met during wedding dance rehearsals, finding their rhythm together long before they were planning a wedding of their own. What followed was a relationship built on movement, creativity, and a shared love for art, culture, and spontaneous adventures—from dance camps in Goa to a sea-facing proposal that felt straight out of a film.
Bringing together Kanchi’s Marwari Jain roots and Kartik’s South Indian traditions, the festivities unfolded across two ceremonies; a traditional Tamil wedding and a Marwari Jain wedding—creating a weekend where cultures didn’t just coexist, they danced with each other.

The Mystique of Meenakari
Some crafts quietly recede into history, and others continually reinvent themselves without losing their essence. Meenakari belongs firmly to the latter. Centuries old yet strikingly relevant, this intricate art of enamelling metal carries a timeless allure that feels both historic and refreshingly modern.

posts.caption:
When Danielle Snyder’s husband got her tickets to her first NBA game in 2021, she faced a relatable conundrum: She couldn’t figure out what to wear.
For Danielle, who has a keen eye for craftsmanship — she runs a jewelry brand with her sister, Jodie Snyder — regular merch with standard team logos was simply not going to cut it. So she chopped up one of her husband’s old Warriors jerseys, stitched it to a plain white T-shirt in a patchwork fashion and covered the number 30 on the front and back in crystals.
Her DIY project would become the catalyst for a new business venture for the sisters: a creative sports merchandise brand @dannijopro. Only two years in, the brand is sold at about half a dozen stadiums across the country and is worn by celebrities as well as a host of athletes’ wives and girlfriends (or WAGs, as they are sometimes called), including Ayesha Curry.
Much other women’s sports apparel is just modified from men’s items following a “pink it and shrink it” model. Read more about how the Snyder sisters charted their own course, at the link in our bio. Photos by @dollyfaibyshev
posts.alt_text:
“Cats: The Jellicle Ball” has received nine Tony nominations, including one for Qween Jean, the costume designer. Our chief fashion critic, Vanessa Friedman, joins our chief theater critic, Helen Shaw, to talk with Jean and uncover some of the show’s hidden references.

At the link in our bio, read our full review of “Jellicle Ball.” Video by Helen Shaw, Vanessa Friedman, Léo Hamelin, Laura Salaberry and Sutton Raphael/The New York Times

posts.caption:
As we approach the Knicks’ first appearance in the NBA finals since 1999, fans with the misfortune of not being named Timothée Chalamet or Spike Lee have been cursing the gut-punching ticket prices for the team’s home games at Madison Square Garden.
But tickets are not the only Knicks commodity to have peaked during the team’s providential playoff run. The market for vintage Knicks apparel has soared, as have the prices. Over the weekend on eBay, a tee from the 1999 finals sold for $350. What is the obsession with vintage merch?
"To wear a tee from the ’90s or the ’00s is to demonstrate (genuinely or not) that you have been with the team through the good times and the bad," our fashion reporter, Jacob Gallagher, wrote. In his latest The Fashions newsletter, @jacobwgallagher talked to vintage clothing sellers in New York about keeping up with the demand. Tap the link in our bio to read the full story and subscribe to The Fashions. Photo by Nathaniel S. Butler/NBAE, via Getty Images

posts.caption:
To celebrate Marilyn Monroe's 100th birthday, 1,037 fans came together to dress as the screen legend in downtown Palm Springs, beneath her giant statue.
In doing so, they set a Guinness World Record in the very place where her career began. Palm Springs — whose identity is inextricably linked with the star — likes to claim Marilyn Monroe, born Norma Jeane Mortensen, and the feeling was mutual.
Participants arrived with Marilyn handbags, Marilyn necklaces, Marilyn earrings, Marilyn umbrellas and enough red lipstick to supply the entire city. Blonde wigs and white dresses were provided by Palm Springs Pride, the organizers of the citywide birthday party. Though all the wigs were the same, no one appeared to be celebrating the same Marilyn Monroe.
At the link in our bio, read about what the cinematic legend means to her fans and see more photos from the record-breaking event. Visuals by @francesca.forquet
posts.alt_text: Photo by New York Times Fashion & Style on June 03, 2026. May be an image of basketball jersey, magazine, sportswear, newspaper and text.

posts.caption:
Anyone walking down the street in the ’90s would not have been surprised to see a passerby toggling between a couple of pagers at once, and maybe retrieving an early flip phone or a Discman from a Kate Spade purse.
But after years of progressive slimming down and merging of our electronics into single devices that do everything, clunky seems to be back. The screens are bigger, the headphones are more obtrusive and, crucially, there are two or even three phones on hand.
It may seem counterintuitive, but many people who carry two cellphones believe it’s the healthier choice, allowing for clearer boundaries between work and life. But the poly phone life comes with the inconvenience of having to actually carry them. And everyone handles it a bit differently.
From stacking to pocket stuffing, tap the link in our bio to see how New Yorkers are managing the multiple-phone life. Photos by @instadickie
posts.alt_text: Photo by New York Times Fashion & Style on June 03, 2026. May be an image of basketball jersey, magazine, sportswear, newspaper and text.

posts.caption:
Every lifestyle entrepreneur needs a home grand enough to serve as an aspirational backdrop. Martha Stewart had Turkey Hill, her historic farmhouse in Westport, Connecticut. Hannah Neeleman has Ballerina Farm, a 328-acre spread in Kamas, Utah.
Nabela Noor found such a place two years ago, an 11,000-square-foot white-brick Georgian Revival mansion, built in 1912, in York, Pennsylvania, the small city where she grew up. The daughter of immigrants from Bangladesh, Noor has been a full-time digital creator for a decade, and her social media followers, more than 12 million of them, have watched her transform her home and grounds with her family.
Now, the mansion serves as the setting for the new Tubi streaming series “Hosted by Nabela Noor,” as she moves her idealized take on family life beyond the social media platforms where she started. The New York Times got a tour of her home and spoke with @nabela about her content, the show and what's next for her and her family. Tap the link in our bio to read the full article. Photos by @schaunchampion
posts.alt_text: Photo by New York Times Fashion & Style on June 03, 2026. May be an image of basketball jersey, magazine, sportswear, newspaper and text.

posts.caption:
Walking through SoHo with Maurice Kamara, you get the sense that he is judging every outfit he sees. Often, he likes to ask strangers — and, increasingly, celebrities — about their outfits on camera, posting the videos on his Instagram account the People Gallery.
At its core @thepeoplegallery_ is a document of personal style. The account has featured Kim Kardashian, Michael B. Jordan and Anna Wintour. His interviews with celebrities come off as spur of the moment, but in reality, they typically require a decent amount of planning — or waiting. “A lot of people don’t know that it’s a lot of work,” Kamara told The New York Times.
But interspersed among the celebrities are video interviews with everyday people whose outfits simply happened to catch his eye. At the link in our bio, more about Kamara’s passion for fashion and how he has built his account into what it is now. Photos by @aundre

posts.caption:
#SadieSink’s custom @prada look styled by @mollyddickson got me thinking - is the Regency fashion era back? Emerging in the late 18th and early 19th century, the empire silhouette was inspired by Greco-Roman sculpture and the cultural changes following the French Revolution. Marking a departure from structured court dress, replacing heavy corsetry and panniers.
The Regency revival can partly be influenced by the rise of #Bridgerton, repositioning the empire silhouette as a modern look. Recently, both Emma Stone and #Zendaya wore @louisvuitton looks reminiscent of Regency wear.
This influential era has always been part of fashion, with designers like #JohnGalliano romantically referencing the empire line waist during his tenure at Givenchy and Dior. #JonathanAnderson’s Dior tailoring, waistcoats, high collars, and elongated coats, is also reminiscent of early nineteenth-century dandy dress.
Images courtesy of Getty / Netflix / Givenchy / Prada
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on May 15, 2026. May be an image of espadrilles, sandals, high-heeled shoes, slingback shoes, toecap shoes and text that says 'Dior Tieat Christian Riger lever Feger ChritianDior hriitian Dior UN Is This Fashion's Most Influential Shoe Designer?'.

posts.caption:
A large part of #DanielRoseberry’s success at #Schiaparelli is, rather than directly reproducing Elsa’s work, he uses her visual language but transformed into something much more contemporary. Many heritage brands get trapped by their archives, endlessly reproducing old signatures, while Daniel mines the archive for concepts while pushing it into a new century.
#ElsaSchiaparelli shocked people because nobody had seen her ideas before, so the most faithful tribute for Daniel is to keep inventing and create things that continue to make people stop, laugh, wonder (or argue) just like Elsa did.
I belive Daniel’s Schiaparelli is one of the rare luxury houses that still feels genuinely artistic. The collections don’t look driven primarily by trends, merchandising, or social-media algorithms. They often feel like couture-as-imagination, which is very close to Elsa’s original vision.
What do you think of Daniel’s @schiaparelli?
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on May 15, 2026. May be an image of espadrilles, sandals, high-heeled shoes, slingback shoes, toecap shoes and text that says 'Dior Tieat Christian Riger lever Feger ChritianDior hriitian Dior UN Is This Fashion's Most Influential Shoe Designer?'.

posts.caption:
Last week, #Dior presented their latest nature-inspired fine jewelry collection by Victoire de Castellane in Venice. Alongside the jewels, I couldn’t help but notice #JonathanAnderson’s unofficial second “couture collection”.
Jonathan created 20 #HauteCouture looks to accompany the jewels, and while his actual second couture collection for Dior is in July, this presentation gave us another glimpse at his work. Letting the jewels take centre stage, the looks seemed to be much more refined, with plenty of nods to his signature draping and silhouettes from Cruise, while steeped in #ChristianDior’s botanical romanticism.
What do you think of this couture collection compared to his debut?
Images courtesy of Anaick Lejart & Dior
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on May 15, 2026. May be an image of espadrilles, sandals, high-heeled shoes, slingback shoes, toecap shoes and text that says 'Dior Tieat Christian Riger lever Feger ChritianDior hriitian Dior UN Is This Fashion's Most Influential Shoe Designer?'.

posts.caption:
#DonatellaVersace’s shows in the 2000s were undeniably iconic, she understood music and fashion worked hand in hand. Her shows felt like stepping into a nightclub. Models stomped, took sharp turns and held eye contact, amplifying the sense of excess and spectacle that defined the era.
My all time favourite was her Fall 2006 runway which featured a mashup of artists like #MichaelJackson, #Madonna, M.I.A., and #Blondie. It Captured decades of pop culture and fashion - exactly what #Versace was, where fashion, celebrity, music, and luxury was inseparable and excessive.

posts.caption:
The Madras High Court has upheld the divorce granted to a retired Army man and held that repeated allegations of extramarital affairs made by his estranged wife to his military superiors, and removing ‘Thali’ or mangalsutra, besides the nearly three-decade separation period constituted mental cruelty under the Hindu Marriage Act.
The court said, "It is known fact that no Hindu married woman would remove the thali at any point of time during the lifetime of her husband. Thali around the neck of a wife is a sacred thing which symbolises the continuance of married life and it is removed only after the death of Husband. Therefore, the removal of thali by the petitioner/wife can be said to be an act, which reflected Mental Cruelty of highest order as it could have caused agony and hurted the sentiments of the respondent."
The couple had gotten married in 1977. According to the husband, the wife frequently quarrelled with him saying that he had illegal contact with several women and insulted him in public. He also alleged that the wife had even sent letters to his superior officers in the Army and lodged a police complaint against him, for which he was convicted for 7 years which was later reduced on appeal.
Source: Live Law
Visit www.shethepeople.tv for more.

posts.caption:
#Opinion | Marriage has always been a very big deal in India, but have you ever noticed the pre-wedding preparations for women? From weight loss drugs to intrusive skincare treatments, bridal beauty packages are booming even more these days, raising important questions about evolving beauty standards
In India, it’s very common to see girls taught to focus on their appearance right from childhood. "Don't go out to play or you'll get tanned." "Don't eat that or you'll put on weight." The same upbringing reflects during marriage. How many men have we seen going to extreme lengths to achieve the perfect "groom" look? Barely any, right?
Beauty standards continuously push women towards extremely harmful treatments for that perfection. What once started with homemade beauty DIYs has now extended to injections and chemicals.
From ‘crash dieting’, where one skips meals and eats extremely low calories, to GLP-1 drugs like Mounjaro, Wegovy, or Ozempic, the pursuit of weight loss has shifted dramatically. The weight loss drug market is forecast to reach 80 billion rupees ($851.79 million) by 2030, according to Reuters.
Read full article by Ankita Kundu @_._i_am_ankita30_._ at www.shethepeople.tv
#bride #wedding bridal glow, bridal lehenga, bridal mehendi, bridal look, indian bride, north indian bride, south indian bride, Maharashtrian bride, Bengali bride, skincare, weight loss, pre wedding shoot

posts.caption:
The aisle is the first thing every guest sees. It’s also the last thing the bride sees before her whole life changes. So why does it still get the least attention in wedding planning? 👀
Save this. Because aisle decor in 2027 is about to be completely different - and brides who plan early are already choosing from these.
The Vintage Rug Aisle - mismatched Persian rugs laid end to end, rich jewel tones, varied patterns. The kind of walk that feels like you’re stepping straight into a haveli that was built just for you. ✨
The Celestial Aisle - fairy lights overhead, iridescent petals underfoot, the whole room turned into a sky. For the bride who doesn’t just want to walk in - she wants to arrive.
The Lantern Aisle - brass and copper, warm flickering light, shadows that move as she moves. Traditional and completely reimagined all at once. 🤍
The Floral Tunnel - not just flowers lining the aisle, but flowers *over* the aisle. A corridor of bloom she walks through to reach the mandap.
The All White Aisle - where every single element disappears into ivory and cream so that the bride in colour becomes the only thing anyone can look at.
The Dried Botanical Aisle - pampas, eucalyptus, wheat, terracotta tones. No fresh flowers. Lasts the entire day. Photographs like a dream.
Which one is walking you to your forever? Comment below - we want to know. 💬

posts.caption:
Kutch doesn’t just set the scene.
It holds it - the pink skies stretching wide over the white desert, the land carrying centuries of craft and culture, and somewhere beneath all of that, a Roka celebration unfolding across two days that felt less like an event and more like a beginning written in the most fitting place it could have been.
That is where Rajvi and Gurbaz’s story started.
And then there was them. ❤️
But what made this celebration truly linger was how thoughtfully it held both worlds - two cultures, two traditions, two families, brought together with the kind of care that makes every moment feel intentional without ever feeling forced. Rooted in love, and in the land that bore witness to it all.
Some celebrations are beautiful to look at.
And some stay with you because of what they meant - the pink Kutch sky overhead, two traditions woven together with grace, and a union that began exactly where it was always supposed to. ✨
Couple: Rajvi & Gurbaz
Location: Kutch, Gujarat
Photographs: @bluswanweddings
Makeup & Hair: @glammsquadbyshradhaluthra

posts.caption:
A Bumble match that ended up at Villa Balbiano.
Sometimes the story writes itself - and then it sets itself on the shores of Lake Como, dresses itself in Tarun Tahiliani, lets the rain kiss the mandap during the ceremony, and waits for the sun to break through right after the varmala. As if it was always going to.
That is Kinji and Herit’s love story.
And then there was them. ❤️
But what made this celebration impossible to look away from was how every element arrived at exactly the right moment - the bold blooms holding their own against the grandeur of the villa, the heirlooms carrying the weight of where they came from, and two people standing on the edge of Lake Como looking like they had always belonged there.
Some love stories begin in unexpected places.
And some stay with you because of how completely they came full circle - from a swipe to vows on one of the most beautiful lakes in the world, with the rain and the sun and the whole of Como bearing witness. 🌟
Couple: @kinjj__ & Herit Vachhani

posts.caption:
Not every bridal look is just an outfit. Some are a whole identity - woven from the soil, the stories, and the centuries of beauty that came before. 🌸
Think of your bridal aesthetic not just as what you wear on the day… but as the version of yourself you’ve always known you’d become the moment you walked into that mandap.
The Rajasthani bride who wears her heritage like armour - all kundan and crimson, regal before she even enters the room. The Maharashtrian bride in her Paithani, where every zari thread holds a generation of grace. The Bengali bride whose shola-mukut and alta-painted feet make her look like she stepped out of a painting that was waiting to be finished. 💫
The Punjabi bride blazing in red and gold, carrying the weight of every celebration her family has ever thrown. The Kerala bride whose ivory kasavu and temple jewellery make quiet the most powerful thing in the room. And the one who chooses royalcore - because she always knew she was built for gold.
It’s never just about the state you’re from… It’s about the aesthetic that was always yours - the one that makes your mother tear up before the baraat even arrives.
Because India doesn’t have one kind of beautiful. It has hundreds.
Which bridal aesthetic speaks to you? Drop your state below. 🤍
Disclaimer: These visuals are for inspiration purposes only and do not belong to us.
#JustSpreehIt

posts.caption:
K-pop fashion trends ✨
With the growing popularity of kpop, more and more Kpop idols are becoming brand ambassadors for luxury brands.
Overall, kpop fashion on the stage largely depends on the song concept and group identity. However, away from the stage idols personalities are brought to the forefront
In the first picture, we see Le seraffim’s HUH YUNJIN wearing a top from Masha-Popova, a Ukraine-born, London-based womenswear designer. As a Giyopo (overseas-raised Korean), Yunjin often blends Korean fashion trends with Western trends, creating an effortlessly cool Gen Z aura.
As BTS’s Golden Maknae, Jungkook has repeatedly collabed with Calvin Klein to express his mature and edgy side. In their recent collection, released in , you can see biker-inspired pieces.
A brand ambassador for nearly 10 years now, the new face of Chanel, Jennie, shows why she was nicknamed ‘Human Chanel’; she embodies it.
Finally, the group XLOV shows how they seamlessly blur the gender binary through their fashion and genderless identity. #kpop #stylecartel #fashion #trending

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.
➡️➡️ If you are a working fashion stylist who is ready to do exactly that, comment STUDIO and we will send you the free trial link to The Studio of Stylist Elixir. Rate guides. Contracts. Portfolio tools. Masterclasses every Monday. Everything you need to build a career that reflects what you are actually worth.
Save this. Send it to a stylist who needs to hear it.

This is not about liking or not liking a collection. This is about understanding what you are looking at.
The Studio of Stylist Elixir is our online membership. Guides. Templates. Live calls. A community of stylists who are learning and building together. Comment ‘studio’ for a free trial.

posts.caption:
Be honest. How many times have you had to explain that your job is not just “picking out nice clothes.” 🙄
You smile and nod at the dinner party while someone tells you they could do what you do. Meanwhile you are the one who got the call at 11pm because the samples did not clear customs. 📦 You are the one who held the whole shoot together when the brief changed on the day. You are the one who went home, lay awake doing sums, and wondered why a job this demanding still feels this uncertain. 😮‍💨
That is the part nobody at the dinner party sees.
They think it is a glamorous day out with beautiful clothes and a coffee. ☕️ They do not see the fifty pulls from twelve showrooms. The personalities you manage on every set. The private client who needs handling with care, the celebrity look that has to be signed off in days, the commercial brief with notes at every stage. They do not see you building a look out of nothing while a whole crew waits on you, then keeping going through the quiet months when the diary goes still and you start to doubt yourself. 🥀
You are not bad at this. You are doing one of the hardest creative jobs there is, mostly on your own, with no one in your corner who actually gets it. 💔
That is the bit I want to change. ✨
✦ The Studio of Stylist Elixir is a paid membership community for fashion stylists who are done figuring it all out alone. ✦
It is the room I wish I had when I started. 🏛️ The systems, the answers, the people who understand exactly what your Tuesday looks like, all in one place, so you stop guessing and start running this like the career it is. 💚
💚 For the stylist who runs the whole shoot, not just the looks
💚 For the one still building when the work goes quiet
💚 For anyone done proving they belong in this industry
▸ Comment STUDIO and I will send you the link to come and see inside. 👀
With love, Em x

Your rate is a decision, not a fixed price. The business side is the actual career, not the chore. Always use a contract, every job, no exceptions. Saying no is how you grow.
Relationships outlast every single job. You do not have to learn it all alone. And the career rewards the ones who build on purpose, not the ones who wait to get lucky.
The lessons are not complicated. The expensive part is the years it takes to learn them
the hard way.
✦ The earlier you start building on purpose, the further you go. ✦

You are turning down work you would have begged for two years ago. Your rate has not moved in three seasons while your work has got better every single one. You are the most experienced person on set and still being booked, and billed, like an assistant. The diary is full. The bank balance is not. Here is the truth nobody says out loud. You will not grow out of this on your own. Not because you are not good enough, you clearly are, but because talent was never the thing holding you back. The business was. The plan was. The room of people who have already done it was.

posts.caption:
Hair colour is often treated as just another beauty routine. But as its use becomes more common, questions about safety are becoming harder to ignore.
As hair colour gets rebranded as self-improvement, self-care and self-expression, dermatologists are raising questions about what’s actually in the dye — from PPD and allergic reactions to scalp irritation, repeated chemical exposure and the limits of “clean” colour claims.
In India, hair colour is also tangled with the pressure to look polished, current and “maintained”. The appointment may feel routine. The risks are anything but.
Read more in the link in bio.
By Ria Bhatia (@riabbhatia)

posts.caption:
This is not India’s first Paris couture moment. It is something more specific: one of India’s most visible couture businesses entering one of fashion’s most selective calendars.
Manish Malhotra (@manishmalhotra05), who began in Hindi cinema costume design in 1990, will present on 8 July at Paris Haute Couture Week. Rahul Mishra and Gaurav Gupta have already shifted the idea of what Indian couture can look like in Paris.
For decades, India’s role in the global luxury system has often been positioned behind the scenes: embroidery ateliers, textile production and specialised craft work executed for European fashion houses rather than presented under Indian labels.
Malhotra brings a different vocabulary: spectacle, celebrity, bridal fantasy and red-carpet scale.
Paris couture has often preferred Indian fashion when it arrives with some degree of translation. Malhotra’s debut adds another perspective to that conversation, bringing a different expression of Indian couture to the calendar.

posts.caption:
India’s classrooms are entering the AI age at massive scale, with 25 crore students and 15 lakh schools across the country.
The promise is curiosity, even though access remains uneven. As a result, classroom reality can look more like copy-paste sameness. CBSE’s AI curriculum is set to roll out from Class 3 in the 2026-27 academic year, pitched as a “universal skill”. On ground, teachers are still dealing with weak guardrails, limited training, rote-learning habits, and students turning to ChatGPT for answers before they learn how to ask better questions.
Platforms like SATHEE promise access and personalised learning. But without stronger systems, AI risks scaling the problems Indian education has been trying to solve for decades. What is unfolding is a cultural reset in classrooms, where the future of learning depends on whether AI expands curiosity or flattens thought.
Read more at the link in bio.
By Arshia Dhar (@urseaa)
#AIineducation #IndianSchools #CopyPasteCulture #LearningWithoutCuriosity #FutureOfPedagogy

posts.caption:
A viral song today arrives already broken into parts: the hook for recall, the lyric for captions, the beat for choreography, the mood for someone else’s edit.
In India, musicians are now writing for the song and the scroll, where a few seconds can push a track harder than an old-school rollout.
The shift has made discovery more democratic and more chaotic. Forgotten tracks return through memes and wedding videos. Official audio can turn clips into royalties.
A song can travel faster than any label budget and still risk becoming just another piece of content moving at algorithm speed.
Read more at the link in bio.
By Karishma Kuenzang (@kkuenzang)
#viralsong #instagramviralsong #viralhits #musicdistribution #culture

posts.caption:
The male body, once allowed a certain laziness, is now expected to show its working. At some point, getting hotter stopped sounding fun.
Looksmaxxing sits at that extreme end. It is male vanity after it has been given a scoring system. In a culture where status, masculinity and identity can feel unstable, looksmaxxing offers the illusion of control.
The manosphere gives that promise its sharper ideological edge, folding self-improvement into fear of rejection, romantic inadequacy, peer comparison and the belief that appearance determines social worth.
Terms like “low-tier man”, “subhuman” and “Giga Chad” reduce human identity into rankings and visual metrics.
“Bone-smashing is dangerous and has no scientific basis whatsoever. Mewing is wildly overhyped; there is no robust clinical evidence it meaningfully reshapes adult craniofacial structure,” says Dr Macrene Alexiades.
Head to the link in bio to read why the future of looksmaxxing may not be its most brutal version, but its most ordinary-looking one.
By Ria Bhatia (@riabbhatia)

Dua Lipa is officially married!!! According to reports (and photos probably already circulating your feed), she and Callum Turner tied the knot in an intimate civil ceremony at Old Marylebone Town Hall, surrounded by a small group of family and friends.
Naturally, the fashion was impeccable. Dua reportedly wore a custom ivory Schiaparelli haute couture skirt suit styled with a dramatic Stephen Jones hat, white gloves, and Christian Louboutin pumps, while Turner opted for classic navy tailoring.
And if you thought that was it, think again. Reports suggest the newlyweds are planning a larger multi-day celebration in Sicily next week, with Villa Valguarnera heavily rumored as the setting.
Congratulations to the happy couple. I’m not jealous at all…

posts.caption:
Safe to say it’s finally summer, and there was a lot you may have missed this past week in fashion. Between Heaven Mayhem’s refreshed seasonal branding, Chanel pulling out all the stops with Jacob Elordi as the face of the new Bleu de Chanel campaign, and Celine continuing to fuel everyone’s seaside daydreams through its latest campaign imagery, the fashion people are being fed right now.
A lot of the industry feels like it’s leaning back into fantasy this season: sun-drenched escapism, cinematic storytelling…
No complaints from me.
#FashionNews #SummerFashion #Chanel #JacobElordi #Celine

posts.caption:
Less than six weeks out from Paris Haute Couture Week, and it’s officially crunch time. Inside the ateliers, it’s all hands: hundreds of hours going into hand-sewing, 3D embellishment, beadwork, and the kind of techniques that don’t shortcut—ribbon weaving, feather work, lace appliqué. Every look is being finished, refined, and reworked until it’s exact.
At the same time, designers are locking in show flow of sequencing looks, mapping pacing, timing changes down to the second. And behind it all, PR is in full operation mode. Translating months of craftsmanship into global visibility, press moments, and cultural impact.
Six weeks. High pressure. Zero margin.
#HauteCouture #ParisCouture #FashionWeek #LuxuryFashion #TheCitizensPoste

posts.caption:
Fatherhood is a new role for the actor who first got noticed for his performance in ‘Masaan’ (2015). In fact, it seems this off-camera Vicky Kaushal (@vickykaushal09) is perhaps closer to Deepak Chaudhary, the character he played in the film. A sensitive, ambitious young man coming to terms with the world around him. That character, Kaushal says, changed him. But not for the obvious reasons.
“Until then success, to me, was where you reached [in life]. It was never about where you started from,” he explains. “That character, starting from the crematorium ghat in Benaras and going on to work as an engineer in the Railways in Allahabad [now Prayagraj]... He had a far bigger life arc than I’ve covered in my life. Till today,” he says, tapping the arm rest for emphasis. “After that I started having so much empathy for those around me, especially those from far less privileged backgrounds, because I don’t know where they have started from.”
@travellerprasad in conversation with the Bollywood actor at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
“When I studied engineering, it was all theory. I can’t repair a thing to save my life,” says Vicky Kaushal (@vickykaushal09), his hand swiping right in the air, as he explains why he took that route. “Back then I would pass this line of car repair shops and I would feel any worker there would be a better engineer than me. So, when I decided to be an actor, I decided to be a boy from those shops. I took acting classes, but I wanted to learn the craft through practice,” he says. “I wanted to first understand the logic of how it’s done.”
With over 20 movies to his credit since his debut, Kaushal appears to have grasped the logic pretty well, especially when you consider the box office. ‘Uri: A Surgical Strike’ (2019), the movie that catapulted him to stardom, made over ₹340 crore, ‘Sam Bahadur’ (2023) cashed ₹127 crore, and ‘Chhaava’, his most recent outing, netted over ₹800 crore worldwide. Kaushal has a lot more in the pipeline, including Sanjay Leela Bhansali’s ‘Love & War’, which also stars Ranbir Kapoor and Alia Bhatt and another movie of epic proportions titled ‘Mahavtar’. How is the “middle-class Punjabi boy” from Malvani via Andheri coping with all the attention?
At the link in bio, @travellerprasad sits down with Kaushal to discuss, film, food, and fatherhood.

posts.caption:
Thirty minutes after this interview was supposed to begin, Vicky Kaushal’s (@vickykaushal09) tall, statuesque frame bursts in through the door at the Matrix office in Khar, Mumbai. “So sorry, bro,” he says, shaking this reporter’s hand with both of his. His day has been busy—his son’s visa was to be sorted, he couldn’t leave his wife alone, and there’s repair work going on in his building, he says, sounding like your average young husband and father with his daily household chores to take care of, not some Bollywood star with a platoon of staff to handle his every whim.
Since becoming a father, Kaushal says he’s come to understand and appreciate “a lot more” of the work his parents did in raising him. “It occurs to me at the most random moments that that [instilling the right values] is now my responsibility towards my son,” he says. “He is going to learn by observing how his parents are thinking, talking, and behaving. So, I’ve realised whatever you want to see reflected in him has to come in your actions rather than words.”
@travellerprasad in conversation with the Bollywood actor at the link in bio.

This launch from @fastrackperfumes feels surprisingly on-point. Their new ‘Carry Your Vibe’ discovery kit turns fragrance into something you don’t just wear, you carry. Literally. The compact format doubles up as a bag charm, meaning your signature scent is no longer a deep-dive into the bottom of your bag away.
Honestly, it makes perfect sense. We’re already personalising everything else we own. Why should perfume be stuck at home? Especially when life currently involves bouncing between work, coffee runs, dinner plans, and approximately six different events a day.
#Ad

posts.caption:
“In 1879, Thomas Burberry invented gabardine, a revolutionary waterproof material that soon outfitted Polar expeditions, leading the British army to commission a coat for WWI soldiers, which would trickle down from officers to civilians and become what we now know as the trench coat.
In 1989, 110 years later, my father walked into the Burberry (@burberry) flagship store in Picadilly, London, and left with, among other things, a classic beige trench coat from the brand.
In 2026, 147 years since Thomas Burberry’s lightbulb moment, I find myself packing that very same coat for my travels.
In recent weeks, all the cool girls have been spotted wearing a version of the trench. There was Kendall Jenner in Milan, who paired hers with a navy sweater knotted over her shoulder and wide-leg pants. There was Daisy Edgar-Jones (bootie and jeans), Hailey Bieber in Australia in a cropped Magda Butrym version, Simone Ashley’s silk Toteme version over a dress in Cannes, and Rosie Huntington-Whiteley all buttoned up in Phoebe Philo in Paris.
Not that the trench ever went away, but it’s safe to say it needs to be front and centre in your closet.”
At the link in bio, @priyankaskhanna on inheriting the coat of the moment.

posts.caption:
After months of cosmic hesitation and philosophical backtracking, we finally witness Jupiter, the great benefic planet, regain its forward momentum. And with that, something shifts. Not dramatically, not all at once, but perceptibly. The fog begins to thin, the narrative begins to move, and what once felt like a prolonged pause now hints at progression. But let’s not confuse motion with clarity.
This month opens on a paradox: a collective yearning for peace set against an undercurrent of unresolved friction. Diplomacy is the keyword being passed around rather liberally, but in true June fashion it’s not the clean, well-articulated diplomacy of certainty. It’s nuanced, layered, and, at times, conveniently ambiguous. Conversations are happening, agreements are being attempted, but the subtext remains just as important as the words themselves.
Click the link in bio for the whole lineup.
~
What are the planets plotting for you this month? Psychic Sheetal Dewan Kapur (@sheetaldkapur) will help you make sense of all the celestial alignments (or misalignments) with our new monthly horoscope.
~

just when we thought the y2k beauty archive had given us everything, halo hair arrived
the trend got a major boost when Rosalía debuted her bleached “halo” effect back in late 2025, instantly putting fantasy hair colour back on the mood board. not long after, striped versions of the look started appearing across Fashion Week runways, proving that statement streaks were officially back.
then came Olympic figure skater Alysa Liu, who brought the trend to an entirely new audience with her striking raccoon-inspired colour placement. interest in the athlete surged by a staggering 39,228% this summer, while searches for raccoon-style hair colouring jumped 598%.
from the runway to the rink, it seems the bold streak is no longer a throwback ,  it’s the hairstyle of the moment

posts.caption:
Model Divyanshu Joshi’s tragic death while on assignment has rightly sparked difficult conversations about workplace safety, professional contracts, accountability and the protections available to fashion industry workers, especially models. As these discussions continue, another pattern has become increasingly visible: the intensity of public criticism directed at the fashion industry itself. Beyond concerns about this particular case, fashion often attracts unusually strong reactions—from accusations of superficiality and privilege to broader questions about labour, power, ethics and value.
Why does fashion evoke such scrutiny? Is it judged more harshly than other industries where commerce, creativity and unequal power structures also coexist? Or does fashion’s influence on culture warrant a higher degree of public examination?
TVOF invites thoughtful views on a sensitive subject. Why is fashion so often put on trial?
Write your views in the comments section.
*Lead image used for representational purposes only.
#indianfashionindustry #modelling fashionabdculture indianfashi

posts.caption:
Beyond one tragic incident lie deeper structural failures.
Barring a few exceptions, fashion modelling in India continues as an informal profession. Models are expected to show up, perform, travel, pose in difficult locations, and assume risk—often without basic protections that most other industries consider standard. Divyanshu Joshi’s death last week in an Ernakulam quarry while shooting for Kartik Research, is yet another wake-up call for fashion brands, production agencies, casting directors, managements and shoot crews. Also for employers. Especially as Joshi was not just a model, but an employee at Kartik Research.
Here is a minimum code of care list.
✅ Risk assessments before official assignments
✅ Safety protocols at outdoor and hazardous locations
✅ Written contracts and clear liability clauses
✅ Medical and accident insurance
✅ Emergency response procedures
✅ Protection against discrimination, harassment and stereotyping based on gender or sexuality
✅ Timely payments
✅ Mental health safeguards
✅ Compensation and acknowledgment in the event of injury or death
Fashion speaks about image and visibility. But what about responsibility? Read the full piece via link in bio.
🖋️ @shefatwork
#thevofashion

posts.caption:
Hi, I’m Veronica 👋🏼 a sewing pattern designer based in Melbourne, Australia.
I create patterns, tools and resources that help you sew with understanding.
My work focuses on how fabrics behave, how garments are constructed, and how sewing connects us to the history of what we wear.
Sewing Patterns
My patterns are designed for versatility, wearability and fun. They include the Hazel, Freya, Odette, Audrey, Hera, Cordelia and the Sleeve Studio — each combining comfort with thoughtful design details. You’ll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.
The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.
The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.
Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
• Shirring Calculator – works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
• GSM Calculator – estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.
My PDF sewing patterns are stocked digitally through @fabricgodmother , @spotlightstores , @thefoldline , @joans.finefabric and @thedrapersdaughter .
Fabric and Accessories
You can shop my fabric designs on @spoonflower and find my illustrated phone cases at @thedairy .
Everything I create is designed to help you sew with confidence, understanding and creativity.
For all enquires email us at hello@veronicatuckerthelabel.com

posts.caption:
The algorithm thinks we want to be a TradWife but it’s totally misreading us 👀
I’ve spent all week writing about why millennial women are pausing on sourdough content, what the algorithm does with that pause, and what we actually want which is not what we’re being sold.
This essay covers haptic data, religious pipelines, the GirlBoss era’s hangover, gentrification in human form, and why I went from TradWife curious to something else entirely.
Full essay linked in bio. Free to read.
Now tell me, when you see TradWife content, what’s your gut reaction?

posts.caption:
The books that are building The Goddess Project.
Six months of research into the history of draped cloth as a political act and these are the books sitting on my desk, on my @kobobooks Libra Colour, and in my @thequirkycupcollective reading journal while I do it.
From Elizabeth Wayland Barber reconstructing what women were actually doing in the ancient world, to Victoria Bateman exposing the inaccuracies in how that history has been recorded, to Emily Wilson (the first woman to ever translate the Odyssey into English) and Stephen Fry making Homer genuinely funny.
Every one of these books is informing a carousel, an essay, or a podcast episode in the Goddess Project series. If you want to read alongside me, this is the list.
The Goddess Project carousels are live on this page. The Substack essays go deeper 👉🏼 link in bio.
Watch the full video breakdown of this list and more books on my YouTube channel.
Paid partnership with @thequirkycupcollective 🫶🏼 Gifted by @kobobooks_au #ad
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on October 23, 2025.

posts.caption:
This is so important for the history of human kind, especially women ❤️
Don’t forget the Gaulish as well. They’re often harder to find than Irish or Welsh mythology because much of it was not written down by the Gauls themselves. When the Romans conquered Gaul, a lot of the oral traditions were lost or blended with Roman religion. It survived mostly through inscriptions, archaeology, and Roman accounts rather than through medieval mythological texts like those preserved in Ireland.
Epona, perhaps the most famous Gaulish goddess. Rosmerta, Nantosuelta, Sirona, Sequena (who I love, goddess of the river La Seine in Paris) reflecting a common Celtic belief that rivers, springs, lakes and landscapes possessed divine feminine spirits.
The Matres or Matronae,they are some of the most fascinating feminine figures in continental Celtic religion. Rather than a single goddess, they seem to embody the sacred feminine as a collective force.
This “triple goddess” motif later appears in many Celtic traditions.❤️❤️❤️
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on October 23, 2025.

posts.caption: Lovely sleeve. I read left to right as is our custom, lol. I prefer the left. It is elevated, whereas the right is flippant and doesn't honor your work - it's more like saying look at my clever funny impression of this work. Also, putting randomly oriented emojis near your photo is not good, because they are cartoonish and draw the eyes away from your beautiful photo. The only thing I didn't like on the left is the description of the shirring as starting at the elbow when it really starts about mid-bicep. I think sewists want the most accurate description. Beautiful work - the dress is wonderful!
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on October 23, 2025.

posts.caption:
They spin the thread. They measure it. They cut it.
Three women have governed every life that has ever been lived and held it in their hands. Not even the King of the Gods could change what they had decided ⚡️
It was always women holding the thread.
The Three Fates is part of the Goddess Project - a series tracing the history of women, cloth, and power across every culture that has ever existed.
New to the series? Start with The First Makers: Ancient Mediterranean & Near East which is also available as a podcast episode on The Culture of Cloth 🎧
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on October 23, 2025.

posts.caption:
“I don’t want to understand everyone; I want a world where I live and let others live. In New York City, I was staying in the middle of a huge building, surrounded by people of different races and religions, and there was no tension whatsoever. Sloterdijk even talks about how we need to learn a certain kind of discretion. That’s what bothers me — the idea that I have to understand your world. How am I supposed to understand your world when neither you understand it nor I understand my own? I’m very superficial in that regard. You have a neighbor — establish some kind of order, with basic respect, and don’t push yourself onto them, nor let them push themselves onto you. I take great pleasure in discretion. Maybe that comes with age.”
For the May Issue of Vogue Adria, we spoke with @SlavojZizeks, one of today’s most renowned philosophers, about the future of the left, the dangers facing modern society, one night stands, and revolution.
Photo: @juergentellerstudio
Creative Partner: @doviledrizyte
Interview: @dazedsparkle
Editor-in-Chief @milandjacic

posts.caption:
Pleasure Principle is a new series of texts by Vogue Adria dedicated to curious and informed conversations about pleasure. A safe space where we talk about the politics of pleasure through essays and interviews, we discuss how pleasure is shaped, experienced, expressed through bodies, culture, power, identity, psychology. We speak with pleasure scholars, activists, erotic professionals, intimacy coordinators, scientists, psychologists, writers, artists, directors — and write from within it, through philosophy, activism, somatics, intimacy, aesthetics, popular culture and psychology of pleasure.
Pleasure is not a side note. It’s the subject. So, let’s talk about it properly. Read more, link in bio.

posts.caption:
Welcome back to #BeautyMarks. This week's best beauty looks were skin-forward: glowy bases, radiant complexions dusted with twinkly highlights, and petal-like blushes. @HavanaRoseLiu was bleach-browed and glossy-skinned, while @EllaBright__ opted for a nostalgic, posey pink bloom across her cheeks. We also can't forget about the tresses—enter @SiennaSpiro and her ’60s-era Twiggy-style up-do with a sweeping middle part, crafted by @jackluckhurst. posts.caption: The hottest fashion trend in New York this summer? @NYKnicks fandom. Vintage jerseys, limited-edition collaborations, custom game-day looks, and celebrity courtside fits are turning basketball into a style movement. As merchandise sells out, watch parties pack venues, and courtside appearances generate millions in earned media value, anything Knicks-related is cultural currency in New York right now. Headed to their first NBA Finals appearance in more than 25 years, the New York Knicks have a deep celebrity following and lots of brand love to cash in on. @CamiFateh weighs in at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Always-on recording of what you look at, pick up, hear, react to, and ask about is now possible thanks to #AI smart glasses. Smart glasses are the perfect vehicle to collect this commercially valuable data, and experts say the devices could rewrite advertising for retailers and brands alike. This "ambient data" collection is Big Tech’s newest obsession — but, as with any data play, it’s not without its risks. Speaking with tech experts, @AmyEOBrien weighs in at the link in bio.
Photo: @mrstreetpeeper

posts.caption:
Today, we launched our latest series, ‘How to Sell Now’, an interactive guide to retail in 2026.
In this chapter, @VogueBusiness explores what makes a luxury product worth buying now. Featuring exclusive data from @VogueShopping, and our own survey, we dive into new consumer behaviors, where shoppers look before they buy, and how brands today are under growing pressure to demonstrate value beyond design alone.
Stay tuned for sneak insights into the full ‘How to Sell Now’ report, which comprises six chapters and is available exclusively for Executive Members via the link in bio. Not an Executive Member yet? Sign up today.

posts.caption:
At a moment when fashion and culture are embracing whimsy once again, Victoire de Castellane’s latest high jewellery collection for @dior feels perfectly attuned to the mood. Her signature suns, stars and fantastical creatures are joined by more personal touches, with exceptional sapphires treated almost like brushstrokes, set against mother-of-pearl, ornamental stones and vivid lacquer. The result is proof that joy can be the ultimate luxury. What’s your favourite piece from the collection? Tell us, below.
#VogueFashion

posts.caption:
Ashna Jain (@ashna739) messaged Aakash Shah first. They had been introduced through their families, but the rest moved on their own terms. The wedding in Jaisalmer took place at Suryagarh and food was one of the clearest ways they brought their worlds together. For the welcome lunch, they brought in Swati Snacks, with a Gujarati spread sourced from Ahmedabad and Mumbai. For the chooda ceremony, Jain leaned into North Indian comfort food, with 15 types of chaat, chole kulche and winter staples. Jain wore Sabyasachi for the wedding and the veil carried a hand-embroidered line: Sada Saubhagyavati Bhava. “It felt like carrying a prayer into the mandap,” she says. To see their wedding up-close, head to the link in bio.
Image credits: @knottingbells
#VogueWeddings

posts.caption:
Fresh from her latest appearance at Cannes, Aishwarya Rai Bachchan (@aishwaryaraibachchan_arb) is thinking about travel in a different way. For someone who has spent years crossing continents for work and life, the focus is no longer just on where she’s going, but how she experiences the journey. It’s also the thinking behind her latest role as Global Brand Ambassador with JW Marriott (@jwmarriotthotels), where the emphasis is on making space for more mindful and meaningful travel experiences.
Image credits: @dirkalexanderphotography
#VogueCulture
